segment-text: SIMON
BEALE
RISEBOROUGH PADDY
STEVE BUSCEMI

segment-speaker: OLGA KURYLENKO ADRIAN MCLOUGHLIN PAUL Written by Armando Iannucci
segment-text: & David Schneider (VERSION 10)
Registered Office: Death of Stalin the Film Ltd
c/o Free Range Films
5 Percy Street Production Office: Death of Stalin the Film Ltd Ealing Studios 1st Floor, Centre Block Ealing Green, London W5 5EP INT. CONCERT HALL - AUDITORIUM - NIGHT
A chamber orchestra is performing Mozart's Piano Concerto No. 23 (2nd movement), featuring soloist, MARIA YUDINA.
INT. CONCERT HALL - DIRECTOR'S OFFICE - NIGHT

A small room looking into the hall. The programme director, ANDREYEV (45) leans back in his chair. Eyes closed, he "conducts" the music with a cheap fountain pen. A technician, SERGEI, keeps an eye on dials and buttons. There's a clock and a portrait of Stalin on the wall. The phone rings. Grudgingly, Andreyev answers.
(snappy) Radio Moscow. Director Andreyev. What is it?
He suddenly tenses, gesturing at the technician to turn down the music. Sergei watches him, munching on an apple.
17 minutes?.....
He gropes manically for a piece of paper.
The pen doesn't work. He shakes it - no good.
(he's not) ..Yes, I'm writing it down..
He smacks the apple out of Sergei's hand and gets him to hand over a pencil.
.. 9?.. Was it a 9, like 'fine' or a 5 like.... (gestures to Sergei) ..hive?
Hello?... Hello? They've gone. He hangs up. He's in a state. Who was it?
The Secretariat of the... General Secretariat. The General Secretariat's.. the General Secretary's Secretaria...
(interrupting) ...Stalin?!
Sergei turns away. He's not getting involved.
I'm to ring back in exactly 17 minutes. 17 minutes from the start or the end? How long did I talk for? HOW LONG?
INT. CONCERT HALL - AUDITORIUM - NIGHT

Some of the audience are lost in the music. A NERVOUS MAN glances at an NKVD GUARD looking at him. Trying to hide his panic, the nervous man looks away then back to the guard... who waves coyly. A woman sat next to him waves back. The nervous man heaves a sigh of relief.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - OFFICE/DINING ROOM - NIGHT

We're on a large portrait of Stalin: handsome, strong, sleek dark hair. We pan down to see the real STALIN - frail, old, grey hair - at his desk. His frail fingers search through lists of names as the Mozart concerto plays on his radio. Humble decor: a few chairs, a desk, a small bed. A short, stocky man with cold eyes behind a set of pince-nez stands on the other side of the desk. This is BERIA.
I put Shteyman on the list. The writer. I know you like his stuff, but...
Stalin thinks for a moment, then shakes his head.
No. Keep him on.
(points at the next name) Shteyman 2, his wife.
Stalin gives a tiny shrug meaning 'yes.'
And Shteyman 3. His brother. On. Better to be sure.

Beyond Beria, through a doorway, a bulky, balding middle-aged man - KHRUSHCHEV, 59 - is entertaining two other bulky men: MALENKOV - 57, - and MOLOTOV - 70. Khrushchev is miming an amusing war incident; people in a panic not knowing what to do with a live grenade.
..they were all jumping around like drunken whores..
INT. CONCERT HALL - AUDITORIUM/DIRECTOR'S OFFICE - NIGHT
From the auditorium, we see the window of the director's office with the figure of Andreyev pacing in frustration.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - OFFICE/DINING ROOM - NIGHT
Khrushchev is miming a man with a grenade in his mouth.
He had this look like: (muffled) 'There's a grenade in my mouth'.
Stalin signs the lists and hands them to Beria.
I'll have these dispatched.
INT. CONCERT HALL - DIRECTOR'S OFFICE - NIGHT
Andreyev stares at the clock. Time has passed; the music has moved on to a new movement.
"It is the greatest honour...' (he bows)
He can't see me bow, idiot.

It's 10.27. Andreyev rushes for the phone, banging his hip painfully on the table. He grabs the phone and dials. The music finishes and we hear applause. He gestures frantically to the technician to turn the loudspeaker off. Someone answers the phone.
Hello?... YES.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - OFFICE - NIGHT
Stalin is on the phone, with the applause playing on the radio and the others laughing drunkenly in the other room. This is Stalin. In the background, Khrushchev is continuing his story.
So I said "You'll have to enunciate". Then-
He mimes the grenade going off, to uproarious laughter.
INT. CONCERT HALL - DIRECTOR'S OFFICE - NIGHT
The applause is loud. Andreyev rushes to a cupboard.
(stammering) Comrade... General... How are things? Sorry... Hold on...
He gets in the cupboard. The phone cord only just reaches.
Sorry again...
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - OFFICE - NIGHT
(interrupting) I want a recording of tonight's performance. I'll send someone to pick it up.
Stalin hangs up. He notices that one sheet of the list of names has been left behind. Beria comes back in.
(picking up sheet) Ah. Don't want them to miss the party, do we?
He heads back out.
EXT. STALIN'S DACHA - FRONT OF BUILDING - NIGHT
Beria divides the lists between some SENIOR NKVD OFFICERS, including DELOV.
(pointing to the list) Shoot her before him but make sure he sees it. And this one. Kill him, take him to his church, dump him in the pulpit.
He's pleased with the idea. He heads back to the dacha.
INT. CONCERT HALL - DIRECTOR'S OFFICE/CORRIDOR - NIGHT
Some MUSICIANS sweep into the Director's office, others are at the entrance out into the corridor.
...she said "I only wear them at
Laughter. Surprise as Andreyev steps out of the cupboard.
Was the concerto recorded? Was it recorded?! Say "yes" to me. Um... No.
Andreyev can barely breathe.
Stop them leaving!
INT. CONCERT HALL - HALLWAY - NIGHT
Andreyev storms along corridor and down some stairs.
Break their legs. Not that, but anything else.
INT. CONCERT HALL - FOYER - NIGHT
Andreyev reaches the foyer and fights against the flowing crowd and in to the hall were some musicians are packing up.
Encore! Encore the whole bloody thing! Don't test me, you fucking idiots. Sorry. You're very talented. But please go back on.
INT. CONCERT HALL - AUDITORIUM - NIGHT

The audience are leaving, among them the NERVOUS MAN. He's keeping his eye on the NKVD man by the exit. Andreyev rushes to the stage, and bangs a cymbal with a flute, knocking aside music stands to get to the front.
(to Security) Lock the doors! Lock the doors! (to everyone else) Don't worry, no-one is going to get killed. But I do need you to stay!
Everyone - musicians, audience - look at him. The nervous man is sweating, terrified. Security guards block the doors. The NKVD man remains inside.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - DINING ROOM - NIGHT A sparsely furnished dining room after a long night of
drinking and eating. Stalin is just sitting down at one end, filling his pipe with crumbled cigarette tobacco. Malenkov tops up everyone's glass.
You know, you put someone's little finger in a glass of water when they're asleep? They wet themselves.
What next? Place a bar of chocolate in their pocket, they'll shit their pants?
It's biology, chief. We did it to Polnikov in Stalingrad.
Beria joins them, picking up a tomato from the table.
Potato doing his war stories again? (looking at his watch, then to Molotov) WELL before midnight. Pay up.
Molotov gets out some cash. They've clearly had a bet.
You're bankrupting me, Nikita. This is a capitalist conspiracy.
What became of that old dog Polnikov?
You want to know where fucking Polnikov is? You want to GO there? Malenkov knows he shouldn't have asked.
Beria puts his arm round Khrushchev.
I love that grenade story. Oh, is there one in your pocket?
Khrushchev pulls a squashed tomato from his pocket. Beria and the others laugh. Stalin too.
(laughs, camping it up) I die for the Motherland!
MUSIC: dramatic orchestral.

Under the music, everything slows down. In slow motion, we see Khrushchev and Beria laughing at each other, like two braying animals. Khrushchev tries to wipe his hand on Beria's jacket, generating more laughter. We can just hear, at a quarter of the speed, Beria and Khrushchev calling each other 'Potato' and 'Tomato' but it sounds like grunts. We see under Beria, the caption "Lavrenti BERIA. Head of Security Forces". And under Khrushchev, his caption: "Nikita KHRUSHCHEV, in charge of Agricultural Reform". MUSIC OUT - Back to normal speed. Malenkov takes another tomato from the table, desperate to be in on the joke.
Take THIS, you bastards! He mimes pulling a grenade pin from it with his teeth. Now doesn't know where to take the joke. Puts the tomato down.
Anyway...better get back to...
Time for a cowboy movie. Who's in my posse?
Everyone tries to look excited.
Oh good, it's Stagecoach again. Yee-hah!
INT. CONCERT HALL - AUDITORIUM - NIGHT
The orchestra are on stage. Half the audience remain. Security guards are at each door. Andreyev at the front of the stage. Maria is nearby at the piano.
Half the audience have gone. The
acoustic will be very dry.
Pull people in off the streets! Fat ones, so we won't need so many! MUSICIAN 1, the orchestra's joker, asserts himself.
I could call my wife? She'd... (indicates "fat") ..."dampen the acoustic".
Laughter. Andreyev turns to the audience, trying to smile.
A TRUMPETER plays a quick fanfare, prompting laughter.
Comrades, shut up! I have great news! Wonderful news.
(to Andreyev) Are you pregnant? The trumpeter parps. More laughter.

Comrade Stalin... (shoots a look) ...loved tonight's concerto and would like a recording of it right away which we don't have for some reason and trust me we will look into why that happened, but meanwhile... the concerto we just played will be played by us again... Now. Please. We will record it and applaud it.

No smiling now. Some of the musicians start clapping, then the audience, security guards, a very relieved nervous man, everyone. It's a huge standing ovation, though you can just make out someone dry-heaving. When it dies we hear Maria...
I'm not going to do it.
You cow hole bitch.
EXT. MOSCOW STREETS/INT. CAR - NIGHT

The cars from Stalin's dacha fanning out from the main car and off on different routes across the city. We see the list from the dacha in the hand of Delov. We stay with one car as it passes several black militarystyle trucks and vans. NKVD men in the truck.
EXT. MOSCOW STREETS - NIGHT
Black marias pull up. NKVD men and Delov get out and start fanning out towards the apartment blocks.
EXT. APARTMENT BLOCK - NIGHT
Pan across windows. Despite terrible noises from below, not one light goes on. We think we see a slight stir in the dark at one window.
INT. CONCERT HALL - AUDITORIUM/DIRECTOR'S OFFICE - NIGHT
Andreyev hurries Maria up the concert hall and into his office. The conductor follows them in. Through the office window, we can see the orchestra and audience watching.
You, Joan of Arc, you want to get killed?
Oh, like my father "got killed"? My brother? Like THAT?
He sits her down in a chair.
You want a family reunion in the mortuary?!
The Conductor is in the background looking unwell.
I won't do it. You can't force me.
(to the conductor) Should we get another pianist?
No, that's ridiculous. The sound would be entirely different. Even Stalin could... "Even".
(frozen in fear) No... I meant...
Even STALIN? Is this office bugged?
(trying to help out) Of course Comrade Stalin will be able to tell the difference. He's a great man, with a great ear for music.
Two great ears.
The two greatest ears in the Soviet Union.
Andreyev comes close to Maria, takes her hands, pleading.
Maria Viniaminovna...
(of her fingers) Don't crush the little workers.
He lets go.
You have to play.
(to the unseen bug) I didn't mean what I said...
(to Conductor) So you said it then. (to Andreyev) As God is my witness, I won't do it. The Lord will see me through.
10000 roubles. Done.
Andreyev hurries Maria out into the corridor, followed by the conductor - who faints, banging his head on a sand bucket.
Who put that there? What brainless fucking fire safety FUCKER put that there?
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - DINING ROOM/OFFICE - NIGHT
It's dark. Khrushchev, Beria, Malenkov and Molotov are watching a John Wayne western. Beyond them, Stalin stands by his desk, looking at papers. Molotov has fallen asleep, with Malenkov next to him.
He gets shot off his horse. (guy gets shot off horse) Yup. Adios.
(loudly, so Stalin hears) Horses are shit, son! You should have commandeered a tank.
You know we produced 5000 T-64 tanks last year?
If I have to watch this fucking movie again, drive one over my head.
I can have that arranged. Ach, little sleep tonight. My men are bringing in lots of lost sheep...
Anyone we know on the list?
(admonishing) Hey, we're supposed to be watching Shitcoach.
They look up to see if Stalin is listening.
(quieter) Any good ones?
One of his guards. Says his wife gave him some slippers to wear so he wouldn't make any noise. The old man's convinced it's so he can creep up on him in his sleep and kill him.
Where will it end? Are we arresting anyone with feet?
Malenkov laughs.
Nikita. I'm having a man shot for "acquiring slippers". Never joke about something so serious.
Stalin comes towards them.
(loudly, to alert Molotov) Ah, you're just in time for the Red Indian attack.
He does a loud, clumsy hand-over-mouth war cry. Molotov wakes and panics.
He.. the horse.. I was.. thinking.. important.
You were asleep.
No. Focussed... on the soundtrack.

He yawns, and we go into slow motion again. Dramatic MUSIC Again. Under Molotov, the caption: "Vyacheslav MOLOTOV. Foreign Secretary". Under Malenkov, laughing, is "Georgy MALENKOV. Deputy General Secretary". Back to normal speed.
(to Molotov) You'll soon have plenty of time to sleep, comrade.
He turns to the others. A look of enigmatic menace.
INT. MOSCOW APARTMENT CORRIDOR - NIGHT
Men in nightshirts are marched out of doorways by NKVD.
EXT. MOSCOW APARTMENTS - NIGHT
Men and women in their nightclothes led into vans by NKVD.
INT. MOSCOW APARTMENT - ROOM - NIGHT
BORIS BRESNAVICH and MRS BRESNAVICH are asleep. There's a loud banging at the door. They switch on the light, terrified.
Don't open. Don't open it!...
They'll kick it down...

Mrs Bresnavich goes to the window and sees A MAN being led away by NKVD OFFICERS. She starts to cry. Bresnavich has pulled on a dressing gown and slippers over his pyjamas. The couple hug and kiss like people who know they may never see each other again.
I love you...
(distraught) Oh my God, your things....
He clasps her hands.
Say whatever you have to say to them. Say it. It doesn't matter.
Bresnavich heads to the hallway. Through adjoining windows, he sees another MAN being led away. Still the knocking.
INT. MOSCOW APARTMENT - HALLWAY - NIGHT
Bresnavich opens the door to a friendly-looking CONCERT DIRECTOR in a dinner suit. In the hallway, two people are being led away.
Comrade. Deepest... (he bows) Radio Moscow requests your presence immediately. Please. You're Moscow's finest and... nearest
(a little desperate) We must hurry.
EXT. STALIN'S DACHA - FRONT OF BUILDING - NIGHT
Beria, Khrushchev, Molotov and Malenkov are walking together to their limos, drunk, tired.
(to Malenkov) Nice work mentioning Polnikov. (Malenkov impression) "Whatever happened to Polnikov? And Trotsky, I liked him."
"I miss the tsar"...
What is this, the Malenkov Pogrom? I'm drunk. I can't remember.
A tip, Melanie. When you go home, get your wife to write down everything you think you said. Then in the morning you know what you're dealing with. Khrushchev's Law!
Good night, comrades! Long live the Communist Party of Lenin-Stalin! Long live John Wayne and John Ford!
Molotov drives off in his limo. He waves. They wave back.
(does throat cutting gesture) Goodbye, old friend. Goodbye forever.
They all register the shock.
Yep. On the list. It would be simpler and cheaper if they just drove straight into a river. Sweet dreams!
INT. CONCERT HALL - MAIN AUDITORIUM - NIGHT
It's fuller now: some in overalls, one with a dirty brush, beside more smartly dressed concert-goers. A BIG WOMAN eats pickled onions. Some are filing up to the balcony.
(shouting at people on balcony) You... no, YOU! Yes, keep going along! Fill it up!
The orchestra warm up. Andreyev starts clapping. The
audience, ushers, Concert Director and security guards join

in. Andreyev wants it louder. The audience oblige. Maria enters, bows and takes her seat at the piano. Andreyev keeps the applause going as Bresnavitch enters with a conductor's baton, still in his dressing gown and slippers.
Shit. Was Chaplin busy?
Bresnavich can't do Mozart! He has no feel for nuance.
He's in his dressing gown. I'd say
Bresnavich turns to face the orchestra and loses a slipper. He gropes for it with his foot as he taps his baton to begin. The orchestra starts playing, from the very beginning.
INT. APARTMENT BUILDING - BEDROOM - NIGHT

A MAN desperately "over-dressing" - putting layers of clothes on top of each other. He grabs a roll of money and stuffs it in his underwear. The door is kicked open to reveal NKVD men. INTERWOVEN WITH:
INT. KHRUSHCHEV'S APARTMENT - BEDROOM - NIGHT
Khrushchev slowly, drunkenly, taking off his clothes. His wife, NINA, sits on the bed taking notes.
I joked about the farmers. Stalin laughed. I joked about the navy. He didn't laugh.
INT. LUBYANKA - CORRIDOR - NIGHT
Beria, yawning, strolls along, cleaning his pince-nez. Behind him an NKVD guard, ILYIN walks with a crying WOMAN in her 20s.
I'll see you again in twenty minutes.

Beria gestures to a cell door and the guard pushes her in. Further back, another door opens and an unconscious prisoner is dragged out by 2 NKVD men. They lift the prisoner's hand up and wave it at the other occupant in the cell - goodbye.
INT. KHRUSHCHEV'S APARTMENT - BEDROOM - NIGHT
Back with Khrushchev and Nina.
I mentioned Polnikov. You fool!
No, I got away with it. Then Malenkov mentioned him again, Stalin hated that.
INT. APARTMENT BUILDING - STAIRWELL - NIGHT
A MATRONLY WOMAN desperately scrubs blood off stairs, crying.
EXT. APARTMENT BUILDING - LANDING - NIGHT
A YOUNG MAN points some NKVD to the front door of a building with a distinctive design.
EXT. MOSCOW STREET - NIGHT
Several people are brought out of the distinctive building by NKVD and put in vans. One is a MIDDLE-AGED MAN, who clocks the young man hiding in the shadows. He knows he's to blame.
INT. LUBYANKA - INTERROGATION ROOM - NIGHT

A FRIGHTENED PRISONER sits on a chair in the middle of the room, hands cuffed behind his back. A door opens and Beria comes in. He stands right behind the prisoner and massages his shoulders, whispering in his ear.
INT. CONCERT HALL - MAIN AUDITORIUM - NIGHT
A serene Maria and sweating Bresnavich bring the concerto to its conclusion. There's a brief silence - relief - then applause. The Big Woman is still eating pickled onions.
INT. CONCERT HALL - DIRECTOR'S OFFICE - NIGHT
Andreyev jumps and grabs the recording.
Shouldn't we check it...? Andreyev is already running out with record.
No time! If it's screwed, so are you!
INT. KHRUSHCHEV'S APARTMENT - BEDROOM - NIGHT
Back with Khrushchev and Nina.
Beria did the tomato-in-the-pocket thing. Arsehole. You'll have to clean the trousers. Then... slippers...
(looking for slippers) I think they're... Shh!
(swaying as he tries to get out of a trouser leg) Oh God, what was the slippers thing? Stalin's slippers?
Molotov's? Fuck. WHAT DOES IT MEAN?
INT. CONCERT HALL - ENTRANCE STAIRCASE - NIGHT
Andreyev manically pelts down the staircase towards the NKVD men, brandishing the new record, followed by his staff.
I HAVE IT! I HAVE IT!...
He sees Pervak ahead, waiting. Andreyev slows to a walk, he's done his duty. The staff follow suit.
(more low key now) I have the recording. We needed to find a new... sleeve.
He looks down at the sleeve. It's plain and white.
A white one. Because the other one was.. blue. It looked very... shit. The delay has been logged.
I've done my duty. Any "delay" is being incurred now By YOU.
A hand takes the record from him. Maria places a folded piece of paper into the sleeve.
I wish to convey a special message from my heart. Andreyev and Pervak both disapproving.
I wish to convey this record to Comrade STALIN.
Pervak and Maria wrestling for the record.
I want Comrade Stalin to know the full intensity of my feelings for him.
No. This is unauthorised narcissism!
He tries to retrieve the note. We can see the record bend. Pervak grabs it back.
(indicates clock) So. The item is in my possession. After a significant "delay". (to NKVD Officer) Log the time.
The officers turn to leave. Maria watches them go. Andreyev drops to the floor, destroyed.
EXT. CONCERT HALL - NIGHT MUSIC.

The crowd, including the nervous man, leave. Spotting the NKVD limousine, Pervak and the other NKVD man outside the main entrance, he grimaces, turns and walks away quickly. Two NKVD MEN suddenly bundle him into a car. It's over.
INT. NKVD CAR - NIGHT The record speeds through the city, on Pervak's lap.
The car passes some frightened people being bundled into a van. There are fewer buildings as we reach the countryside.
EXT. RUSSIAN COUNTRYSIDE - NIGHT
The car drives down a forest road. Two barriers are lifted in front of it by Army soldiers.
EXT. STALIN'S DACHA - NIGHT
The car speeds into the grounds of Stalin's dacha.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - HALLWAY - NIGHT
The record travels under Pervak's arm, past various soldiers. A SERGEANT and another SOLDIER stand and salute. The door opens. A frail hand carefully takes the record.
The door closes. The officer breathes a sigh of relief. MUSIC ends.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - OFFICE - NIGHT

Stalin removes the record from its sleeve. Maria's note falls unseen onto the table. He puts the record on. The stylus scratches, plays. He sees the note. Opens it and reads. Stalin laughs. Then chokes. Silently shaking, he collapses. His body convulses, he's suffocating. SLOW-MOTION. Title: "JOSEF STALIN, General Secretary of the Central Committee of the Communist Party". As he falls, urine streams out. We see the note fall from his hand and flutter down. We hear Maria in V/O.
Tyrant! Murderer! You have sinned against God and against His church. You have betrayed our nation and destroyed its people. Repent. And beg for His forgiveness, while you can.
Stalin's face hits the floor. A pool of urine spreads out.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - HALLWAY - NIGHT
The sergeant and soldier on guard outside hear the noise. The sergeant does a "he's drunk" gesture.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - OFFICE - NIGHT

Stalin on the floor, twitching. The record on a loop. A bar of the concerto cuts hard into scratching, repeating again and again. They really should have checked the recording. A puddle of urine on the rug.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - HALLWAY - DAY
More time has passed. The soldiers sit there, bored. MATRYONA PETROVNA (77) arrives with tea. Knocks. The soldiers share a glance. A beat. She opens the door, enters...
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - OFFICE - DAY
The room's a mess. Matryona tuts.
Comrade Stalin. How many times? You cannot drink like a young man...
She goes to scream. We cut before the sound comes out...
INT. LUBYANKA - INTERROGATION ROOM - DAY
C/U POV of Beria's face. We're a prisoner being interrogated.
I just need some names. Each name you give me is one less bit of you I'll cut off. Shake? Oh, you can't.
We cut wide as we hear a knock on the door. Beria, bloodstains on his shirt, stands over a beaten prisoner tied to a chair. Ilyin is in the room.
BERIA (CONT'D)
SLIMONOV, an NKVD officer, enters. Someone in another cell shouts 'Long Live Stalin', before a gun shot.
Beria turns to Slimonov, who looks down at the prisoner.
Don't worry about him. Those ears are full of blood anyway.
Comrade Stalin is very ill...
Beria gives nothing away. In the background, we hear another voice shout 'Long Live Stalin' followed by a shot.
Tell them... Beria is on his way. They are NOT to touch a thing. NOT to call anyone else. Understand?
Slimonov gives a nervous nod.
Tell me you understand my instructions?
I understand. Your instructions.
Beria goes to leave.
What's your name, lieutenant? (very nervous) S...s...limonov, sir.
S...s..limonov. Be here when I g..g..get back.
Beria leaves.
INT./EXT. LUBYANKA - CORRIDOR/BACK YARD - DAY (CONTINUOUS) 52
Beria walks through the Lubyanka corridors with his second-incommand, KOBULOV. In the background we see a man being carried by two NKVD, tied to a heavy wooden log.
Abramovsky is close. But weak. (off Beria's look) Tomorrow, maybe.
Have his wife moved into the next cell. Work on her until he talks. Make it...noisy.
In the background, the man and log are tossed down some stairs. We can only vaguely make it out, but enough to know.
If he doesn't...
They go up some stairs. A GUARD brings something for Beria to sign. He signs.
He talks, he doesn't talk. It ends the same way. Shame. Mrs Abramovsky has been MOST co-operative so far...
INT. LUBYANKA - FRONT RECEPTION - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
They walk to the front doors.
(chuckling) Very loyal. Some women will do ANYTHING to get their husbands
(fondly) Yes. And she did 'everything'. God bless the Union for bringing me so many devoted wives who go like sewing machines.
EXT. LUBYANKA - FRONT OF BUILDING - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
They've reached Beria's car. Khrustalyov holds the door for him and he gets in.
How's married life?
Very good, Comrade Be...
Watch that wife of yours. They can all go astray, you know. (to driver) To his dacha. The limo drives off, as a van pulls up behind.
INT. KHRUSHCHEV'S APARTMENT - KITCHEN - DAY
A kitchen in a modern apartment. Khrushchev is in his pyjamas, hungover, reading Nina the notes she made.
"Grenade funny Beria tomato pocket funny slippers question mark." Jesus, Nina, is this code?
You woke me up to slur this nonsense and it's MY fault?
"Molotov C-H-H-H-H...". Holy Hell,
She does the throat mime.
(looking out of the window) Oh God, yes. Beria. He...
Below in the courtyard Malenkov is seen hurriedly leaving.
Something's happened.
The phone starts ringing.
(on phone) Hello.... When did it..? (to Nina) Trousers! (on phone) I'll be there faster than a speeding bullet!
Nina helps him haul his trousers (from the same suit as the previous day) over his pyjamas.
EXT. STALIN'S DACHA - PERIMETER GATE - DAY
The front gate is opened. Beria's car sweeps through.
EXT./INT. FRONT OF BUILDING/BERIA'S CAR - DAY
Beria's car drives up to the building. He gets out. Soldiers salute him as he enters the building, confident, determined.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - HALLWAY - DAY
The Sergeant and soldier snap to attention, suppressing fear.
Were you on duty when he was found?
Yes, sir.
No-one is to come in, understood? Yes, sir.
Remain at your stations.
The younger soldier is struggling to hold himself together. Beria goes inside and shuts the door.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - OFFICE - DAY
Behind the door we hear a whimper as the soldier panics. Oh God...
SOLDIER (O.S)
Stalin is still on the floor. Matryona is stroking his hair.
They told me not to get a doctor.
You did well, Matryona Petrovna. The Central Committee will handle things now.
So you will get a doctor?
I AM the doctor. I'll see to everything.
She gets up and goes. He watches her shut the door, turns back to Stalin.
It smells like a Baku pisshouse in here. Greetings, by the way.

He kneels down, tries to reach into Stalin's trouser pocket but it's soaked with urine. He hesitates then goes for it. He pulls out a key and wipes his hand on a handkerchief. Beria sees Maria's note, reads it and pockets it.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - SMALL ROOM - DAY

Beria heads into an adjoining smaller private room. He unlocks a drawer with Stalin's key. He takes out a set of keys, heads to a bookcase, opening it to reveal a safe. He opens the safe and removes a pile of folders. He closes the safe, locks it, and returns the keys to the desk drawer. He pulls out a few files and then heads to the window with the rest. He can't open it. It's jammed shut. He pulls up his trouser leg to reveal a knife in a sheath strapped to his calf. He tries cutting at the dried paint round the edge of the frame. The top half comes down a fraction, leaving a gap at the top. Beria taps the window to attract Khrustalyov's attention and gestures for him to come over. He drags over Stalin's rickety chair and clambers onto it.
EXT. STALIN'S DACHA - FRONT OF BUILDING - DAY
With Khrustalyov. Beria shout-whispers through the window.
Khrustalyov! Khrustalyov!
Khrustalyov hears him and rushes over.
INT./EXT. STALIN'S DACHA - SMALL ROOM/FRONT OF BUILDING - DAY

Beria perched on the arms of the chair. He waves for Khrustalyov to get higher. Khrustalyov clambers onto the ledge as Beria feeds the documents through the window. Beria wobbles, falls back a little but averts disaster by pulling at the curtain. Khrustalyov can't suppress a smile.
Take the papers you blockhead. Or I'll cut your eyes out one at a time, so you can watch it happening.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - OFFICE - DAY
Beria throws the remaining papers into the stove. Phew. Sits in Stalin's chair - nice. Luxuriates in it. A KNOCK. Beria quickly kneels by Stalin, taking his hand. Come in.
He realises the hand is covered in piss but has to keep hold. Malenkov walks in.
Is that... I'm guessing that's....
He rushes over and kneels in the urine. He springs back up.
Yes. He's feeling unwell. Clearly.
Oh God, he's irreplaceable. How can we possibly... is he still...?
Malenkov indicates "alive?". Beria nods.
As Deputy General Secretary I must now take his place, and...
"Take his place." But you just said he's irreplaceable.
"Take his place" as in "assemble the Central Committee". Of course.
Good. I was testing you. Get used to that sort of challenge. (indicates Stalin) What next, Boss?
Well... This is a medical decision. We should get a doctor.
If only we hadn't put away all those highly competent doctors. For treason. You remember.
(uncomfortable) I do. They were plotting to poison him...
That's right. You collected the evidence...
Yes I did. (a beat) Are you still testing me?
Khrushchev bursts in.
This is calamity! Calamity!
Khrushchev rushes over to the body. He kneels, just missing the piss, pressing his face into Stalin's chest. Then...
NOOOOOOOOOO!!!
Jesus, Nikita!
Sobbing, Khrushchev grabs Malenkov's arm and stands up.
My friends. Comrades-in-arms...
He hugs Malenkov who also starts to cry. Beria feels he should join in the hug. It doesn't come naturally.
Which doctor have you called?
The subject is currently under discussion.
As Acting General Secretary, I say the Committee should decide.
But our actual General Secretary is lying there carpet-bombing the room with piss. I think he's saying, Get me a fucking doctor.
We wait until we're quorate.
Quorate take a running fuck. The
room is only 75 per cent CONSCIOUS. Beria notices pyjama bottoms beneath Khrushchev's trousers.
Why are you in your pyjamas?
Because, I act, Lavrenti. Decisively, and with great speed. I don't... (to Malenkov) (MORE)
...hang around looking like a fucking ghost, farting out committees.
The Central Committee is important. That's why we're on it.
(to Malenkov) I said you'd be tested. And just now you're being tested by a shouting man wearing pyjamas. (RE pyjamas) Got a nappy under those, too? (RE Stalin) Too late for him!

The door is flung open by MIKOYAN, 58, well-dressed gangster type with a lit cigarette between his lips, and BULGANIN, 58, a head-down timeserver. With them is KAGANOVICH, 60: big, a bruiser, drunk. We go into slow motion as all three try to squeeze through the door. We hear their shocked reactions slowed down.
(in slow-motion) Papa!/Comrade!/Shit! Underneath them, the captions: "Nicolai BULGANIN. Minister
for Defence"; Mikoyan: "Anastase MIKOYAN. Vice-Premier, the Council of Ministers"; "Lazar KAGANOVICH. Minister for
We go back into real time as they squeeze around the body.
No! I love him too much!...
Bulganin falls to his knees, sobbing. His arm finds Kaganovich. Khrushchev puts a hand on them both. Bulganin reaches up, tugging Mikoyan's jacket. Mikoyan hates that.
(warning) Suit.
This is compelling drama, but we do need to get him into bed. (nods at Malenkov) Take the head. Why?
You're Acting General Secretary.
(round the other side) Can't be any heavier than my fucking heart. Khrushchev grabs Stalin's feet. There's now too many on the
non-urine side. Only Kaganovich is on the urine side.
He gestures for Mikoyan to go to the other side. Grudgingly, he does so.
Better. More balanced.
Malenkov doing some light stretches.
What the hell are you doing?
I have back trouble. The head's the heaviest part...
I think actually it's the stomach and the, you know... Tackle? Arse.
Can we stop haggling like a market full of fishwives and do this! Malenkov joins the others. Khrushchev pushes Mikoyan to the
upper half of the body so he has to lift near the urine.
Ready. 3...2... Malenkov joins in loudly. ...2..1...
Khrushchev allows Malenkov to finish it alone. ...Lift!
They lift Stalin and carry him to the door, feet first.
He's a heavy man. I mean...
You think Stalin is..."too heavy"?
It's a compliment. Gold's heavy.
You'd know. You looted enough of it, you saucy little pirate. (winks at Bulganin)
Now we're all cosy and quorate, I hereby propose we get a doctor. They go through into...
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - HALLWAY - DAY
We're a way down the corridor as they manoeuvre Stalin from the office to a door opposite.

The best doctors are in the gulag. Or dead. Because they tried to kill Stalin. So any doctor still in Moscow is NOT a good doctor. The Soldier and Sergeant are further down the hall, trying hard to avoid seeing what's going on.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - BEDROOM - DAY
They get him through the door. It's a struggle.
What are people's thoughts on getting a "bad" doctor?
What if he recovers and finds out?
Khrushchev stops so they all have to. He unconsciously gestures with Stalin's feet as he talks.
If he recovers, then we used a good doctor. If he doesn't recover, then we didn't but he won't know. But if we don't get a doctor...
People will say WE poisoned him.
They fucking will as well. Murmured agreement. They start moving towards the bed again.
(to Malenkov) What was the name of that woman who denounced the doctors? Timashuk.
Yes. Are you still in touch?
Well, we don't go skating together.
We could get her in.
(really?) She sounds a keen helper, but... a negative individual.
She's got everything we need for this situation: a desire to survive and blowjob lips.
Well, she's got my vote.
Yes. Let her find us some doctors.
If it ends badly, we pin it all on Lady Sucky Sucky.
And we shoot her.
That would work. (to Khrushchev) See, we're better as a committee.
They reach the bed. Stalin's feet are over the pillows.
We cannot display the Father of the Nation upside down. Let's turn him.
They turn him. Stalin's head narrowly misses a bedpost.
Watch his head!
Let's be careful! He's not a sack of coal!
Malenkov winces: his back hurts. They move Stalin onto the bed, right across Mikoyan who's bent over backwards. The urine-stained trousers pass right across his face and brush his expensive clothes.
Mind the...! Achtung, achtung!
Mikoyan is freed and Stalin's down. Khrushchev stares at Stalin in disbelief.
He looks ready now.
I need a vodka.
I need a wash.
(re suit) Fine weave this is. Never get it out.
My pocket is still full of tomato.

Everyone leaves. Beria hangs back. Khrushchev stops and watches Beria. He's just standing, looking at Stalin, face blank. He brings his face right up close to Stalin's, staring at him, and pokes his stomach.
You have a nice, long sleep, old man. I'll take it from here.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - BEDROOM/DINING ROOM - DAY

The committee members except Malenkov and Khrushchev stand round a drinks cabinet stuffed with bottles - too many of them. Bulganin struggles to pull out a bottle of vodka. Khrushchev walks in, placing his folded pyjamas down on the table.
I feel like a midwife, ha ha! (pulls one out)
It's a boy!
Bulganin is filling glasses.
Remember? He made us all guess the air temperature outside? Loser sat in the snow, bare ass. Molotov... They all laugh.
(little girl voice) "I'm the Foreign Secretary. Not an Emperor Penguin".
(little girl voice) "My cock will snap!"
(re Beria) Careful. All these little girl voices...
Where is Molotov, by the way?
He's on a list. The latest list... The laughter stops. It's a big name to be on a list.
What about Svetlana and Vasily? They'll want to see their father.
No. I don't want them contacted until we have a plan. We...
(interrupting) We stand strong. We burn bright. Because if....if our Great Beacon is extinguished, if the Great
Helmsman dies...
(interrupting back) Stalin is not dying! Do you hear me? Stalin will never die! (MORE)

That remnant of a man there, that pathetic old lump of flesh in his piss-stained jodpers - that is not Stalin! Stalin is the Union! The Party! YOU are Stalin. I am Stalin. Understand? I am Stalin. The people are Stalin. And they will see this when we tell them. WHEN we tell them. So, don't any of you fucking say Stalin is dying! He lives!
Silence. The tension's broken by Malenkov coming in.
Timashuk's organising the doctors. (innocently) What did I miss?
EXT. MOSCOW PARK - DAY MONTAGE of Moscow park exteriors: fountains.

An old man, LUKOMSKY, with his small dog on a lead. A black maria stops. Lukomsky realises it's for him. He starts to run as best he can. Heading past the fountains. Some NKVD MEN, including Delov run to catch him. He abandons the dog.
Please... I've done nothing wrong!
They catch him. An NKVD OFFICER is waiting with a doctor's white coat, a stethoscope and a doctor's bag.
Put this on.
I retired 6 years ago.
Put it on!...
I can give you names?

The NKVD men put the coat on him and open the van doors. Inside are other terrified DOCTORS, including a WEIRD-LOOKING TALL DOCTOR. LIDIYA TIMASHUK, an attractive woman of about 40 gets out the front and she and an NKVD officer chase the dog. We cut before we find out if they caught it or not.
EXT. STALIN'S DACHA - FRONT OF BUILDING/GROUNDS - DAY
RED ARMY SOLDIERS with dogs patrol. Others are around, and some NKVD visible in the background. Khrushchev and Kaganovich walk along a path.
Poor Molotov. He loves Stalin. Stalin kills his wife. He still loves Stalin. Stalin arrests him.
Stalin told me it would be Bulganin next.
Really? I heard Mikoyan. God he was good.
(testing Kaganovich) No, he was paranoid. I think he was on the point of arresting his own reflection.
Kaganovich doesn't respond. They walk in silence. Khrushchev
So. Who'll be on the next list? (indicates "me and you")
Look, nobody wants the future of the Union to be Beria and Malenkov
staring at each other across a fucking graveyard. But what...
Reform. Change. We really want millions in camps? Look, we're not the only ones worried about Beria. We should sound out Mikoyan and...
That's factionalist talk, Nikita.
They stop. In the distance, Malenkov is pissing against a tree, with Beria very close to him.
You want factionalism? (points across) Beria's playing Melanie like a fucking musical saw...
We join Malenkov and Beria by the tree. Malenkov's pissing.
When I piss, I try to make eye contact with an officer. Ruins their day!
We need more security in Moscow. Did you hear that pinhead Khrushchev undermining you about the doctors? Remember: Stalin chose YOU.
Khrushchev. Talking goat. Don't worry, Lavrentiy. "Mother Russia" is safe in my hands...
You call it "Mother Russia"?
Beria indicates his penis. Malenkov laughs. We're back with Kaganovich and Khrushchev. They walk on.
I'm pulling Stalin's children in. Now. Keep them close.
Vasily will be lying face down in a ditch full of vodka. But Svetlana..
The people LOVE her. I'm getting her...
You heard Beria. We don't get them on board until there's a plan...
I just TOLD you what my... There's a shout from the courtyard outside the house.
Comrade Ministers! Svetlana Alliluyeva is here!
They look up to the house. Stalin's daughter, SVETLANA ALLILUYEVA (30), is at the top of the slope next to PERVAK.
(to NKVD Officer) Where is he? Is he OK? Who got here first? Who's in charge here?
All four committee members start cutting across the woods. Malenkov and Beria spot Khrushchev and Kaganovich.
(shouting) Svetlana! Hello!
(running) Svetlana! (to Malenkov) They're trying to cut you off!
(running) What? Those bastards!
(running) Shit, the race has started. Fucker's halfway round the track before they've fired the pistol. (shouts) Hello!
Malenkov trips a little and has to stop running. Beria's the first to reach Svetlana. She's been crying.
Has he said anything? Is he... is he able to speak?
Beria catching his breath, doubled over. Svetlana consoling.
It's a shock for all of us. Is Vasily on his way? (to everyone) Has my brother been told?
Beria sees Khrushchev is close.
(panting) Svetulya... I'll take you to him..
Beria puts a proprietorial arm around her. They head to the house as Khrushchev and Kaganovich arrive, breathless.
I'm so sorry, Svetlana...
He tries to take her hand, but Beria shepherds her inside.
Someone contact Vasily! He must be here!
(shouting after her) I'll do it! I'll get him! Don't worry... darling! (fuck no, not that) Svetlana!
(tetchy) Yes, see to that could you, Comrade Khrushchev?
INT. ICE HOCKEY RINK - DAY

A training match. The players are clearly second-rate. One of them - BOBROV - is terrified. All up at one end, with three trainers lobbing at them. Watching them, drunk and angry - VASILY DZHUGASHVILI (30), Stalin's son, in his Air Force uniform. A COACH is with him.
When we play Hungary, are we allowed to use guns?
Comrade General, they're the best I could find since the plane crash.
Stop saying plane crash! There was never a plane crash!
Vasily watches the players scuttle around on the ice, and takes a swig from a hip flask.
Soviet planes do not crash, and Stalin's son does not fuck up.
We still have Bobrov. He wasn't on the plane.
What plane?
(shouts) Bobrov!
Briefly focussed, Bobrov sweeps past the other players, then completely misses the goal. He looks to Vasily, terrified.
Jesus, he's petrified. He's leaving
a trail of yellow ice.
A sense of other players chipping the ice or miss-hitting.
You're the coach. Coach them so they're as good as the dead team was. Or I'll have you killed. OK? Yes, yes.
Three NKVD OFFICERS, including TARASOV step in, watching Vasily from afar. Vasily spots them.
Shit... Do they know?? (shouting at the players) Play better now! PLAY BETTER!
A player crashes into the hoarding in front of Vasily.
General? You are to come to your father's dacha -
That plane should never have taken off. You think because of who I am I can predict ice storms?
The officers share a look.
Has something happened to our national hockey team? No.
(indicates rink) See? No. But ask yourself - might something happen to YOU?
The officers look over at the players, and watch one of them
The NKVD men nod to each other, grab Vasily and pick him up.
What the fuck..? My father will have you saddled and ridden to Siberia! You have no manners, you rude fucking cunts.
EXT. STALIN'S DACHA - FRONT OF BUILDING - DAY
The black maria from the park pulls into the courtyard. Delov and the other NKVD get out and open the doors. The scared shabby doctors emerge. One is very young. Lidiya gets out the front of the van.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - DINING ROOM - DAY
Beria is by Svetlana who's crying. Khrushchev offers a chair. No, no.
Khrushchev goes to sit then thinks better of it.
He's with good people, my dear. They're the best... The shamble of doctors leave the bedroom with Malenkov, and
line up in front of the politburo, Svetlana and Matryona.
Beria got the doctors.
No, that was Malenkov.
Svetlana, would you like to sit down?
Really. No.
(offering his chair) Have mine.
(cross) The men always want me to sit down. NO.
Svetlana regrets her scratchy response, whispers to the men.
Thank you for your concern.
Lukomsky nervously reads from a paper. Lidiya is beside him.
After examining Comrade Stalin we've come to the unanimous conclusion that... (struggling to continue) ...he's...
(nods to Lidiya) Please. Put him out of his misery.
Lidiya takes the paper off him.
Comrade Stalin has had a cerebral
hemorrhage. The right side of his body is paralyzed.
Does he have any chance of recovery?
It's, um... It's hard to say...
Beria goes right up to him, and speaks quietly, calmly.
Relax. I'm not going to kiss you. Will he recover? Yes. Or no.
Lukomsky manages to mouth the quietest "No" ever.
(loud, to the others) It's over!
Shocked silence. Beria gets his hat and prepares to leave. Discretely, a few of Beria's people (NKVD) can be seen hovering at the edge of shot, and down corridors, waiting for his signal.
Now it begins. I cry for Stalin, I cry for all of us...
Beria's people just as suddenly disappear. Beria turns to Svetlana. She looks numb.
Courage, little bird. We're here for you.
Khrushchev realises he should maybe have said this first.
Yes. Most of all, we cry for you. All Russia feels your sorrow. (awkwardly) Little... bird.
Beria starts to leave again. His 'people' appear again discretely.
I must get back. Prepare the nation for this...trauma.
Stay with me, please. I feel alone.
Beria looks torn: he needs to go to Moscow to take control.
I could go to Moscow. I'm... now General Secretary if "he" is not...
(fuck you, Beria) I'll stay with you, Svetlana. The old man would've...
Oh, THANK you Comrade Khrushchev.
(fuck you, Khrushchev) No, I can do things here. Here is perfect. (to Lidiya) Timashuk. Come. Beria's people disappear again.
He and Lidiya leave. Kaganovich is crying into Stalin's hand.
(quietly, to Kaganovich) While Beria has his celebratory thirty-second fuck, I'm going to find out who our friends...are you even fucking listening?
Kaganovich nods, still sobbing.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - HALLWAY - DAY
Lidiya follows Beria down the corridor, past the Soldier and
(to Lidiya) Stay here. Shut your ears.
He goes inside Stalin's office.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - OFFICE - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
Beria shuts the door behind him and punches the air with joy, trying hard not to scream and shout. He picks up the phone.
(phone) Moscow is to be sealed off. Send in more NKVD. All army stations replaced by NKVD. And initiate the new lists. (ends call, sotto to self) Reset your watches, ladies and gentlemen.
INT. LUBYANKA - OFFICE AND CORRIDOR - DAY

Kobulov hands a list to an NKVD OFFICER who salutes, takes it along a corridor to a SOLDIER who salutes and marches to a cell. The door's unlocked. A PRISONER is being interrogated. The soldier shoots him dead and leaves. The NKVD INTERROGATOR is left stunned as we hear O.S a succession of unlocking doors, yells and gunshots.
EXT. STALIN'S DACHA - PERIMETER GATE - DAY

FIVE SOLDIERS on guard. Smoking, relaxed. An ARMY CAPTAIN watches as NKVD MEN get out of a truck. An NKVD OFFICER hands the Captain some orders. Captain doesn't like what he sees. One NKVD man pulls out bottles of vodka. Soldiers look to Captain, who reluctantly nods, snatch the bottles, and amble into jeeps. The army leave. The NKVD are in charge.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - OFFICE - DAY
Beria opens the door. Lidiya steps in and shuts the door. He looks at her for a moment.
Over his desk? As usual? She walks to the desk, bends over it. She's done this before.
Stalin is dying. And all you do is present me with your cold vagina? How shockingly, shockingly inappropriate, Lidiya.
She's surprised, shaken, but tries not to show it.
Oh, don't worry. You'll be thoroughly "seen to" later...
A horrible, courteous smile. He indicates "go". She leaves.
INT/EXT. STALIN'S DACHA - HALLWAYS/FRONT OF BUILDING/GROUNDS

Lidiya walks downstairs and out the building. Two NKVD Officers with documents look up at her. More trucks arriving. All exits routes blocked. We see a GUARD recognise her name on a list. Lidiya runs in panic into the woods. We go with her. Beria watches from the front of the dacha. An EXPLOSION. Guards cock their guns. Bulganin rushes out.
A mine? Are we being attacked?
(shrugs) Perhaps an animal. (to an NKVD Officer) You have your orders.
He turns and goes back inside.
INT./EXT - VASILY'S LIMOUSINE - DAY
Vasily's limo drives through the countryside. Vasily sits between the NKVD officers in the back, drinking. No-one says anything. Then...
Do either of you play hockey?
INT. KREMLIN - STALIN'S OFFICE - DAY

The room is big and spartan. Simple furniture, wall maps. An outsized chess set. The lights are on. The locked door CRACKS as an NKVD OFFICER forces entry. A SENIOR NKVD OFFICER enters, switches off the light.
EXT. RED SQUARE - DAY
From across the square, we see the light go out.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - OFFICE - DAY
KHRUSHCHEV steps in. It's dark; the curtains are shut. He checks the safe: it's locked. He looks on the desk, opens a couple of drawers.
I have every right to be here!
Khrushchev jumps. He didn't see Svetlana sat in the darkness.
(Thinking of an excuse) Your father kept some excellent vodka here.
And Vasily probably drank it. Oh, Nikita. Who can I turn to now? Mama killed herself, Papa's dying, Vasily's...
(relieved) Yes! I was also looking for you to tell you that I've sent for Vasily!
He's not sure whether to put a comforting hand on her or not.
I'm here. No matter what happens, I'll make sure no harm comes to you or your brother.
Who said anything about harm? Did someone tell you we're in danger? Tell me!
I didn't mean...
(panicky/angry) Who wishes harm on me?
Nobody. Nobody. Anyway, they'd have to get past ME.
People would harm the children of Stalin? Even though he's...
She loses it. A gush of tears. Khrushchev hesitates, settles for an awkward, comradely grip on her shoulders.
Your father can never die. Stalin is you, Stalin is me, Stalin is the Union.. so cannot die!
What are you saying? He's dying.
He is dying...
He casts his eyes down, spots a bottle, grabs it.
Svetlana, just...be strong. And... (out of platitudes) Try not to be afraid.
I wish I knew what not to be afraid of.
Nothing. Trust me.
An awkward double-fisted "be strong" sign. He hurries out.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - DINING ROOM/BEDROOM - DAY

A vast buffet is added to by a CHEF covered in medals. There are empty vodka bottles everywhere. Smoke hangs in the air. Beria is idly playing billiards, watching a YOUNG WAITRESS. He whistles her over, takes a large tomato from her plate. He bites into it, keeping eye contact. Juice runs down his chin. Kaganovich is near the bed, drinking. Mikoyan's on the other side of the room with Malenkov, whose hair is over-groomed.
You had your hair fucking embalmed or something?
Fuck you. There'll be a lot of photos. I can't let him or the Party down.
Khrushchev walks in with the vodka, annoyed with himself. He goes to the bed and tries to take part of Stalin's hand. There are only fingers left; Kaganovich is holding the rest.
(quietly) Did you speak to Svetlana?
(indicates bottle) Went very well. She gave me this. (off Kaganovich's look) A good sign. Vodka's ALWAYS a good sign.
You're right. Beria's taking over. Another 9 NKVD divisions in Moscow. More on the way. Army's out.
He's moving fast. Bulganin comes in.
Got to unload to reload.
You have the bladder of a sparrow.
But the penis of a horse. Nobody laughs. Mikoyan has joined Beria.
(to Bulganin) You ARE the penis of a horse. (to Beria) Tough old bastard. Thought he'd just kick Death in the balls.
Beria puts a reassuring arm round Mikoyan's waist.
Mikoyan pulls a crushed tomato from his jacket pocket. He looks angry, volatile.
Piss and tomato AND Stalin dying. This is a nightmare.
Beria's about to apologise. Mikoyan waves it away, heads out. Lukomsky enters with two other doctors, who wheel a hefty piece of equipment with a very squeaky wheel.
Who ordered the Wurlitzer?
It's... it's a respirator... American...
(gobsmacked) You brought a capitalist machine... in here?
He brought it. It's from his hospital!...
It was his idea...
Khrushchev joins Beria and Malenkov while Kaganovich berates
So, you had time at your hairdresser's to mobilise the NKVD? What?
I did it. Look, people will need... (makes fist) ...reassurance.
So why switch the lights off in Stalin's office at the Kremlin? What?
Ah... I know what happened. I asked for the place to be secured, and the dolts have turned the lights off.
Why not tether an airship above Red
Square with "HE'S DEAD" painted on
Take this Uncle Sam shit away! (unplugs the machine)
He can't breathe!..
Svetlana grabs the plug and puts it back in.
(puts cigarette in his mouth) It's a good thing he can breathe. We're all agreed on that.
Malenkov checks no-one is looking and quickly heads out.
EXT. RED SQUARE - SQUARE IN FRONT OF STALIN'S OFFICE - DAY 83
The same long shot as before. This time the light turns on.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - HALLWAY - DAY
Later. Beria is having a cigarette in the hallway. Suddenly there are cheers from inside the room.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - BEDROOM - DAY
Beria hurries in. The committee and Svetlana are around the bed, weeping, laughing, holding Stalin's hand.
A miracle! Stalin is invincible!
The Man Of Steel! He lives!
The Boss is back!
What? What's happened?
Stalin's eyes are open. Beria moves the others out of the way and takes pride of place right by Stalin's head.
That's impossible! And wonderful! Long live the Union!
Stalin meets Beria's eye. Then points at Khrushchev.
Maybe he's appointing a successor?
(a little bit pleased) Do you think?
STALIN'S POV: his finger passes across all the committee members. The finger stops, points to Matryona.
He points past her, to the wall. They all turn to look at a painting of a young girl feeding a lamb through a bottle.
Maybe he's saying: "I am like this lamb and you, my children, have given me life."
No. Stalin's the GIRL, nourishing the Lamb - the Union - with MarxismLeninism.
My father is not a girl!
He's the milk!
YOU'RE the tit.
Maybe he just wants a drink...
Everyone agrees. Matryona offers him some water. Stalin lowers his hand. Beria goes to Lukomsky, cowering at the back. He shakes the doctor's hand but doesn't let it go.
You are a hero, Comrade Lukomsky.
(terrified) I... Sometimes this happens... And... I'm incredibly pleased.
I won't forget it.
Beria glares at him - it's a death sentence - then leaves.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - HALLWAY - DAY
Beria paces. Agitated, defeated. For the first time, we see him troubled.
(sotto, to self) Live, or die. This is just... dithering. The original soldier and sergeant are still there, exhausted.
Sir, we have remained at our post as you ordered. Might we respectfully...
Beria turns round to look at them but..
Beria's brief smile. He notes the sergeant has spotted it. Beria darts back inside.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - HALLWAY - DAY
We hear a loudspeaker voiceover as Beria walks. ANNOUNCER (O.S)
Stalin...
EXT. GULAG - PRISON YARD - TWILIGHT

A brutally cold, frozen, rugged environment. Low, stone buildings. PRISONERS listen, shocked; many upset. One of them is the Nervous Man from the concert. NKVD GUARDS look to each other. ANNOUNCER (O.S)
INT. KREMLIN - STALIN'S OFFICE - DAY
An NKVD man munching on a salami, burning papers in a bin.
EXT. RED SQUARE - SQUARE IN FRONT OF STALIN'S OFFICE - DAY 91
The same long shot as before. This time the light turns off. ANNOUNCER (O.S.) Chairman of the Council of Ministers, has died after a serious
INT./EXT. STALIN'S DACHA - OUTHOUSE/SIDE OF DACHA - DAY
We see Stalin's dead face, filling the screen. A small medical saw is seen starting to cut into the skull.

Wide to see we're in an outhouse near the dacha. A doctor cutting into Stalin's head with an intense grinding noise. Laboratory and medical equipment surrounded by spare tyres, car engine etc. Stalin's body is laid out. Lukomsky and the doctors, NKVD OFFICERS and committee members are there.
(wincing) NOOO!/Fuck my boots!/Whoa!...
Sorry, boss! I wish I could take it for you!
(to Malenkov) We need to think about the Presidium!
(reacting to the body) Yes. God. Succession. The Committee should. Oh. Fuck. Notify. No. That's his... must we see this?
Regrettable! But important this happens quickly! Transparency!
The double doors at the front of the outhouse are hauled open. It's Vasily, drunk.
What are you doing to my father, you jackals! The very young doctor is peeling back skin on Stalin's skull.
Murderers! You'll kill him!
Beria gestures to the others: he'll deal with this.
Vasily, your father is dead and...
Vasily draws his pistol.
Dividing the spoils? Leave his brain alone! How old are you?
Beria nods at the NKVD. An NKVD man tries to grab Vasily's gun but he fires it.

Everyone dives for cover. Malenkov runs out of the door and pulls it shut. Bulganin and Khrushchev try to follow but Malenkov's holding it shut from the other side. Khrushchev crouches against the door. Mikoyan stands fearless. NKVD men try to subdue Vasily. He struggles, firing his gun.
Here is your fucking... harvest!

They drag him away from Stalin but he fires more shots. They hit Stalin. His head rocks forward as if the shots have jolted him into life for a moment. The NKVD get his gun off him. We hear Svetlana's voice.
Vasily! Vasily! Stop!...
She's come into the garage. Her voice stops him struggling.
Tiny child doctors are cutting up Papasha! They're filling his brain with American lies!
She grabs his face, looks right into his eyes.

(very quiet) He's gone, Vasya. Just you and me now. Be careful. Remember what Papa said. Wade gently across the river because... (rhythmically digging into his face) It. Is. Full. Of. Snakes. And. Crocodiles.
Vasily nods. He's heard her. Svetlana looks at the NKVD men.
(trying to act normal) Someone could have told me what to expect. Next time, tell me.
Beria nods to the NKVD, who let him go. Malenkov opens the door and comes back in.
(to Malenkov) I understand wanting to leave. But why hold the door shut? Who does that?
Cover me, I'm getting Papa!
Suddenly, Vasily grabs for his gun. Malenkov immediately closes himself outside again.
(to Malenkov) Off you go, kitten!
The NKVD men struggle with Vasily, force him to the ground and sit on him. It's not dignified. Svetlana looks ashamed.
(close to Vasily) Moron. I'm not part of your suicide pact. You want to die? Shoot yourself.
You will regret this! You are sitting on fire! Fire!
He's distressed. We all are. (to doctors) Is it done?
One of the petrified doctors nods.
It is finished. Let's go. And someone do something about all of this - (waves vaguely at Stalin) - then take it to the Kremlin.
Where now for me? And my brother?
My men will bring you to Moscow.
I would be honoured to escort you.
Svetlana senses Beria's antipathy to Khrushchev.
Thank you. But no, I'll go with Beria's men.
Beria kisses her and marches out into the courtyard.
Our precious cargo. (shouting) Khrustalyov! My car!
The other committee members look at each other for a beat, then rush off after him. Suddenly everyone's in a hurry.
We need equilibrium. Soon.
"Equilibrium", bollocks. Give me chaos every time. Got more meat on it.
EXT./INT. DACHA - FRONT OF BUILDING/BERIA'S CAR - DAY

Beria arrives at his limo. Khrustalyov opens the door and he gets in. An NKVD officer is in the driver seat, Khrustalyov stays outside. He nods to his boss and heads into the dacha. Inside the car, the young waitress Beria saw earlier is cuffed to the car door. She's frightened. He smiles at her.

I know. Sad times. The car starts but the courtyard's a logjam of vehicles all trying to get out. Other committee members hurry to their vehicles, drivers holding open the doors for them. Svetlana heads with an NKVD man to her limo. Horns start to blare. Political manoeuvering. NKVD men trying futilely to direct. Then, in the chaos, NKVD men emerge carrying a plain wooden coffin. They load it onto the back of a truck and climb in. Beria gets out of his car. Stands solemnly. The others are obliged to get out and show their respect. The NKVD clear a path for the truck and escorts. Beria's car following. He's won the scramble. The others start their cars. Horns blaring, cars jostling...
EXT. STALIN'S DACHA - FRONT OF BUILDING/ROAD - DAY

The last of the limos drive away. An empty road. Silence. Then - a rumbling, getting nearer. A CONVOY of NKVD trucks coming up the road, through the gates, into the courtyard. Armed NKVD MEN pour out, occupying the house and grounds. Kobulov barks into a loudhailer.
Comrades! We share your grief! You will assemble in the courtyard for
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - VARIOUS LOCATIONS - DAY
Busts, portraits, furniture, files, are removed, and medical equipment from the outhouse. Stalin's turntable, the record still on it, is loaded into a box, nailed shut.

Some STAFF are already in a line in front of the house, others are brought out by the NKVD, including the chef and the doctors. Some have belongings, some nothing. A lot of orders are being shouted. People are frightened, confused. A supposedly dead Stalin, in a dressing gown and slippers, is marched out. The chef turns to his confused neighbour.
His lookalike. I think his contract just ran out.

The LOOKALIKE is taken round the corner to be shot. But the SOLDIER executioner can't do it. Looks like HIM. Someone quickly throws a cloth over the lookalike's head. Good. Bang. A second lookalike is marched out. Another, third lookalike, clearly a younger, fitter man, makes a break for it. Close up of a young, fit Stalin running for his life away from the dacha. But we cut away before any mine explosion. In fact, the explosion doesn't come...
INT/EXT. STALIN'S DACHA - KITCHEN/FRONT - DAY
Delov and Two NKVD OFFICERS search the kitchen. They discover Matryona, crouched between two gas stoves, her eyes shut.
Hey, you! On your feet!
Matryona is hauled up and dragged out the front door. Now we hear and just about see a mine explode in the woods.
INT. STALIN'S DACHA - HALLWAY - DAY
The soldier and sergeant are asleep. Khrustalyov appears with Pervak.
The soldiers dozily comes to attention.
At ease. You can stand down now.
There's food in the kitchen.

They head down a long corridor with a kitchen at the end. Then the sergeant comes back into view, collapsing to the floor, unconscious. NKVD arms reach into the doorway framing our view and drag him out. The other soldier runs across the doorway followed by two NKVD MEN. It's messy. The soldier may have gotten away into the woods. We don't know.
EXT. STALIN'S DACHA - FRONT OF BUILDING/GROUNDS - DAY

Loaded trucks pull out of the courtyard. We are with Matryona as the truck moves off. From her pov we see Khrustalyov join Kobulov in the courtyard. They get smaller and smaller in the distance. Kobulov shoots Khrustalyov in the head. Other landmines go off.
INT. KHRUSHCHEV'S APARTMENT/MALENKOV'S APARTMENT - EARLY EVENING
Khrushchev is chopping vegetables. A tired Bulganin is sat at the kitchen table.
So... Beria's introduced 'special measures' to 'reduce the panic'? Carrots.
He looks at Bulganin, meaningfully, as if sounding him out. Bulganin passes him a couple of carrots.
It's good to prepare, get everything ready.
He looks at Bulganin, meaningfully, as if sounding him out.
Are we talking about Beria or the stroganoff?
Khrushchev goes over to his washing machine.
I built a big factory making these.
Khrushchev turns it on. It makes a terrifyingly loud noise.
(shouting over it) If Russia was a noise, then that would be it.
Not so good for washing clothes. Very useful for "conversations"..about Beria.
A pause.
Look, it's important someone takes control.
Someone like Beria? The conversation is suddenly significant. Bulganin comes up
to Khrushchev so they can talk quietly.
It should certainly be someone who has the support of all parts of the party.
Like Beria?
Well certainly one of us.
So... maybe not Beria?
You're very good with that knife. It's ...
(interrupting) I'm saying... maybe not be Beria... We should get rid of him.
Yes. Yes! And soon! Punish him! Stick him in a room with twelve big hungry, horny Serbians!
Suddenly the washing machine comes to a juddery stop, interrupting Bulganin. The men stare at one another in panic. Khrushchev tastes a spoonful of food, not yet ready to eat.
Mmm. That is good. Delicious.
The machine starts again. Khrushchev spits out the food. Feh! Raw!
We should take acting classes. If Stalin hadn't killed all the actors. (MORE)
Look, I want to come on board, but we don't have the numbers. Suddenly the phone rings. Has someone been listening in?
Tentatively Bulganin answers as Khrushchev adds some of the vegetables to a pot on the stove. We see Malenkov, Mikoyan standing next to him, by the window of his apartment.
Are you being factional?
We're cooking some food.
Khrushchev grabs the phone.
I can see you, you know.
We cut to reveal the two apartment windows look on to each other, very close in distance. We see Khrushchev in one window, Malenkov in the other.
Looks like factionalism to me.
Khrushchev grabs a spoonful of stew and stands at the window theatrically eating it.
Tastes like lamb stroganoff to me.
Khrushchev hangs up.
Listen, I'm acting secretary general and I'm - oh, he's gone.
Jesus, squeal any higher and you'll burst your hymen.
They're being factional So are we.
I'm Acting General Secretary. How can I be plotting against myself?
Khrushchev closes the blinds.
On the committee it's you, me, Kaganovich. Three against three. But there's also Molotov.
But he's on the... (mimes "headshot") ...list.
We tell him - Beria put him there. Beria killed his wife. Molotov's revenge gets us FOUR.
Khrushchev goes through to another room, and peers outside through a window. His POV: in a different block to Malenkov's, Molotov stares out of his window, grief-stricken.
(epiphany) He's at home. I'd better get over there before Beria and his sausagefaced clowns do.... The washing machine stops again.
(furious, to the machine) You piece of metal shit! (to Bulganin) Look after the food...
He rushes off, full of urgency.
What do I do? Add salt, right? That's what people who can cook do.
We hear Khrushchev shouting back as he goes.
Recipe book's under the sink!
EXT. APARTMENT BLOCK - COURTYARD - EARLY EVENING

Khrushchev hurries down the stairs, out across the courtyard to Molotov's stairwell opposite. The elevator's at the second floor and rising. He's out of breath but he's on a mission. He takes the stairs.
INT. LUBYANKA PRISON - CELL - EARLY EVENING
Prisoner POV of Beria's face. He's close again. Too close. He's quietly singing a traditional Russian folk song.
"In a field a birch tree stood..." No? Come, your voice was once so beautiful, Paulina Molitova.
He clicks his fingers, "remembers".
Oh yes. Meant to say. Stalin's dead!
We open up to reveal a woman - POLINA - in her late 50s, sat in a tiny, barren cell. She looks haggard, wrecked.
(shocked) No! Our Stalin?
Yes, "your" Stalin. The one who put you here. The waitress and an NKVD GUARD are also in the cell.
Beria hauls Polina up. She doesn't resist.
Come! Let's smarten you up. (to waitress) Sorry, darling. Work. I'll make it up to you. (to guard) Fetch the mattress and have her
He walks Polina out while Ilyin and another NKVD Man bring in flowers, a rug and a jug.
INT. LUBYANKA PRISON - CORRIDOR - EARLY EVENING
Beria leads Polina down a corridor. An NKVD GUARD comes the other way, with Slimonov, handcuffed.
S...s...limonov, isn't it? Yes, sir.
F..f..f..farewell, Slimonov!
Slimonov is led away.
INT. APARTMENT BLOCK - STAIRWELL - EARLY EVENING
Khrushchev wheezes up three flights, pausing for breath before disappearing onto Molotov's landing.
INT. APARTMENT BLOCK - LANDING - EARLY EVENING

Khrushchev, exhausted and sweaty, reaches Molotov's door and knocks. He tries to compose himself but looks quite ill. Molotov opens the door. He's been crying. Khrushchev barrels through, pulling Molotov with him.
Christ, you look like you're about to be bulldozed into a lime pit.
INT. MOLOTOV'S APARTMENT - TOILET - EARLY EVENING
A few moments later. Khrushchev is over the toilet bowl. Molotov is stood behind him.
Do you want me to hold back your hair? Polina always used to do that for me.
I... wanted to invite you to tomorrow's committee meeting...
(snaps back) Why wouldn't I be at the meeting?
Khrushchev flushes.
Beria and Stalin put you on a list.
(shocked, hurt) Stalin? I must have wronged him so badly...
Are you cracked? Beria wanted you out. But listen. We can honour his legacy by making sure Beria doesn't...
Oh! No, no, no. This sounds like factionalism, Nicky. Stalin didn't like...
(loses it) Stalin's dead. It's over. We have to act.
(crying) I can't believe he's gone... Khrushchev tries to flush the toilet again. The cistern
hasn't filled up yet. He tries a couple more times.
It takes a while to...
Khrushchev gives up, keeps his voice down instead.
Come on. That fucking slug Beria had your wife killed. He can't be trusted...
INT. MOLOTOV'S APARTMENT - TOILET/CORRIDOR - EARLY EVENING106
Molotov and Khrushchev walk back towards the living room.
She betrayed Stalin and she betrayed me.
Housekeeping!
INT. MOLOTOV'S APARTMENT - LIVING ROOM - EARLY EVENING
Molotov and Khrushchev come through into the living room to find Beria. Everyone's surprised.
We were just chatting about Polina.
Well, would you believe it?..
The treacherous bitch.
...or: a wronged woman who was framed.
Come on, Stalin found her guilty! She collaborated with the zionists.
Polina's at the doorway. Only Beria can see her. This is not going to plan.
She was a criminal. I'm glad she's dead...
Molotov notices her in the doorway. They look at each other. The barriers drop - it's clear he still loves her.
(hasn't seen her yet) She plotted to poison Stalin...
He sees Molotov heading towards Polina. He can't believe it.
Is it really you?
(full of love) I'm back, Vyacheslav.
They hug, emotional. Beria enjoys it. Khrushchev tries to dig himself out of his hole.
But the evidence was flimsy. No witnesses, no corroboration... No...
Khrushchev gives up. Beria puts his arm round Molotov and Polina. It becomes a group hug.
(to Molotov) I kept her safe. For you,
(to Polina) And I kept HIM safe. For you, Polina.
Beria throws a victorious glance at Khrushchev. Molotov breaks down, overwhelmed. Khrushchev tentatively joins the group hug.
Well done. Well done, everyone.
INT. LUBYANKA - CELL - NIGHT MUSIC: RACHMANINOFF 'HAIL, O VIRGIN MOTHER' FROM 'VESPERS'
The young waitress from the dacha hunches on the bed of the spruced up cell. The door opens. Beria is there with flowers.
Hello, my sweet.
He moves to the table, where a bottle of wine has been uncorked. He sniffs it lasciviously, eyeing her legs.
Mm. Everything at room temperature. Oh, lighten up. It could be worse. Be warned.
INT. APARTMENT BLOCK - KHRUSHCHEV'S FLAT - NIGHT

A miserable Khrushchev eats the stroganoff, occasionally looking out across the courtyard at the Molotovs, arm in arm. Nina has a mouthful. Grim. Khrushchev sees her disgust, silently picks up both plates, scrapes them into a bin.
EXT./INT. LUBYANKA ENTRANCE ONTO STREET - NIGHT
The waitress' frightened PARENTS wait. She appears distraught, stoical. An NKVD GUARD hands the parents Beria's bouquet. They hurry off.
ARCHIVE MATERIAL 2.
RUSSIA IN MOURNING. A red flag with black border flies above the walls of the Kremlin. Beyond it: Moscow, with more redand-black flags.
EXT. KREMLIN/MOSCOW STREET - DAY
Our footage: NKVD ARRIVING IN VEHICLES, TAKING UP POSTS/MAKING ROADBLOCKS ON MOSCOW STREETS. MUSIC ENDS.
EXT. KREMLIN - STREET - DAY
A Zis limousine arrives at the Kremlin.
INT. KREMLIN - ROW OF 3 INTERCONNECTING ROOMS - DAY
Beria walks jauntily through connecting doorways from the far end.
INT. KREMLIN - NEAR END OF INTERCONNECTING ROOMS - DAY

Malenkov being made up to look like a leader. He's uncomfortable under studio lights as the PHOTOGRAPHER snaps away and STAFFERS bustle in and out with business. Two MAKEUP ARTISTS blotting and repairing his make-up.
Comrade, I've scheduled telephone calls later with...
Then tell me LATER.
Beria arrives, looks at Malenkov and laughs.
Almost perfect. Perhaps... an apple just here, in the mouth?
Chuckling, Malenkov wipes his face with a towel.
I have something to tell you. (over shoulder) I'll be back shortly!
A smiling Malenkov grabs Beria and walks stiffly with him into the middle room. He drops the smile to show his fury.
Don't you ever, EVER, do that again. Humiliate me in front of...I'm the General Secretary!
(deferential) I'm mortified. Genuinely.
Looking my best for the People is a duty.
Malenkov walks through the middle room and into the third room. His walks still seems a little stiff.
Of course. Shall we...
(spotting Pervak) What now?
Apologies. The seating plan for...
Give it to Blavatsky. He's an expert at sitting on his ass.
Shall we get onto..
Yes, the committee meeting. Item One. The lists. These arrests and "interrogations". Do we just take them down a gear, or....
Hold off altogether?
What, "freeze" them?
"Freeze"? Excellent thought. Yes, we could freeze arrests, maybe even release some low-level prisoners?
Oh, fuck me. Really? Is this what the old man would have wanted?
Stalin was radical and popular.
Liberalisation WOULD be radical.
Now Delov has edged a little way into the room.
I WILL ADDRESS WHATEVER HORSESHIT YOU HAVE, PRESENTLY!
No, this is something I wanted to show you.
Beria walks stiffly to the third room. Malenkov follows, as if he was going to do anyway. Delov produces on a stand an iconic photograph of Stalin hugging a young girl on a balcony.
I thought, a photo of you and the girl. Continuity?
(delighted) Good idea. We show how we're taking Stalin's humanity forward...
(to Delov) Find this girl.
They leave the room.
INT. WEIRD-LOOKING KREMLIN ROOM OR CORRIDOR - DAY
Moments later. Malenkov and Beria walking to the meeting.
My chief concern is admitting we were wrong. All those arrests. And deaths...
We were being loyal. I promoted Stalin's truth without question. I can promote a new truth without blinking.
An unblinking beat.
So, I'll push on with the releases?
Alright, but proceed carefully. Of course.
Fuck this up and I'll have you shot. Beria says nothing.
Just joking. I was testing you.
Is that a corset?
ANGLE on Malenkov's body. A GIRDLE is apparent.
It's a girdle. Different thing altogether.
You wear it well.
But not worth raising with the Committee.
EXT. KREMLIN - STREET - DAY
Several more Zis limousines arrive at the Kremlin.
INT. KREMLIN - MEETING ROOM Ndeg1 - DAY

The committee sit at a table in an impressive, high-ceilinged room. Malenkov is at the head of the table with his new hairdo and a smart new outfit. The room is dominated by a giant portrait of Stalin.
So, in accordance with... (re portrait) ...his will, I propose Comrade Malenkov be named Chairman of the Council of Ministers. And General Secretary of the Party.
(pompous nod) And I propose Comrade Beria for First Vice-Chairman, Council of Ministers. Those in favour. Beria, Malenkov and Mikoyan instantly put their hands up.

Wait. Those are two separate proposals. Comrade Beria is incredibly busy combining his roles as Minister for both the Interior and Security. It would perhaps be kinder for someone else to carry the happy burden of Vice-Chairman.
I thank Comrade Khrushchev for his concern but I can assure you that I'm capable of doing two things at once.
Well, it would actually be three things.
Let's move to a vote. (to Beria) On both proposals?
Beria gesticulates as if to say 'Up to you'.
Yes, on both. Me as General Secretary and Comrade Beria as ViceChairman. All those in favour?
Malenkov, Beria and Mikoyan raise their hands. Molotov joins
Grudgingly, Khrushchev raises his hand.
...unanimously. Next...
(interrupting) We need someone to take charge of the funeral.
What about Comrade Khrushchev?
(looking at Beria) Where's this come from?
Yes, good thought.
I formally propose that Comrade Khrushchev be given the honour of organising the funeral.
(to Beria) But that will tie up all of my time, won't it?
If I can do three jobs at once, you can at least do two.
But I need to be...
(interrupting) You wanted to honour his legacy. You told me last night, in the bathroom.
Molotov winks at Khrushchev: he's doing him a great favour.
All those in favour..
Again, Molotov, Beria, Malenkov and Mikoyan raise their hands. Kaganovich decides to raise his hand.
(justifying himself) You'd be good at it.
Bulganin raises his hand.
Carried... U... una... Then, grudgingly again, Khrushchev. MALENKOV (CONT'D) ..carried unanimously.
Thank you. I shall remember this.
Beria looks to Malenkov.
(trying to be formal) Comrade Beria.
Beria clears his throat, looks extravagantly at ease.
I propose a halt to deportations. The release of some existing detainees. And the suspension of arrests officially sanctioned three days ago...
Molotov, Khrushchev, Kaganovich and Bulganin are shocked.
You dare wipe your shitting arse on Stalin's lists!?
Let's all stay calm... although what you just said is utterly ridiculous.
(angry) One of you look me in the eye and tell me you're not heartsick at the relentless, pointless arrests. Death after death after DEATH.
A charged beat as he looks around the room, defiant.
Gentlemen, I believe we must embrace a new reality.
("wanker" mime) Yeah. "Embrace" it...
How are we going to explain all the people you very comprehensively arrested in the first place?
If there were errors, if people misguided us then we punish those who did the misguiding...
Arrest anyone who carried out a wrongful arrest? Lavrentiy, come on. We ALL signed off on that shit...
(thinks he can be commanding) Stalin destroyed the status quo and built a new one. He was radical.
This is deliberately being rushed through. We don't have time to..
I thought you were in favour of a more liberal approach, Comrade Reformer.
Khrushchev remains silent, roiling with conflicting emotions.
We must step back...
All those in favour...
(interrupting) ...of pausing the arrests and executions and so on?
Malenkov and Beria raise their arms. Mikoyan, reluctantly, joins them. Bulganin, Khrushchev and Kaganovich look at
I have always been loyal to Stalin. Always. And these arrests were authorised by Stalin.
The rest of the committee follow Molotov's lengthy logic with increasing frustration.

But Stalin was always loyal to the collective leadership. That is true loyalty. But he had an iron principle, undeviating, strong. Shouldn't we do the same, and stick to what we believed in? No. It's stronger still to forge one's own beliefs into the beliefs of collective leadership. (a beat) Which I have now done.
Molotov raises his hand. Kaganovich and Bulganin raise theirs. Finally, Khrushchev raises his hand.
Good, I'll oversee...
(interrupting) Unanimously.
I'll oversee the releases. We have unloosened the Union's great corset. Nikita, you've some swatches and bouquets to look at. A funeral doesn't organise itself.
We stay on Khrushchev as Molotov leans over. Malenkov prattles on in the background.
(To Khrushchev) You've won! Do him proud.

(in background) Beria, I want you to put together a communique for the press. I'll supervise the transfer of the body. Comrade Bulganin, tell the army; Comrade Mikoyan, the factories and the unions...
EXT. GULAG - PRISON YARD - DAY
Twenty prisoners, including the Nervous Man, lined up to be shot by 5 GUARDS and CHASOV.
Long live Stalin!
Comrade Stalin's dead. Comrade Malenkov's in charge now.
Long live Mal...
Chasov shoots him just as an NKVD officer, SHULGA runs up.
Stop shooting!
Chasov turns, but fires off one more shot. It kills the Nervous Man, who slumps dead in the background.
(indicating list) Amnesties effective immediately. Orders of Comrade Beria. We're pulling out.
They hurry off to join their colleagues. The NKVD's mobilising, heading off. The prisoners are left, confused.
INT. HOUSE OF THE TRADE UNIONS - HALL OF COLUMNS - DAY

Close-up of Stalin's face in his coffin. Pull out and high to reveal enormous hall with people bustling, making last-minute preparations.
