The UNDERTAKERS' finishing touches: doing make-up, hair, moustache, polishing buttons. Huge flags hang from the ceiling. More are being rigged. Swathes of red, and flowers, everywhere. Beria and Malenkov stroll along the other side of the hall.

Interrogations are on hold. First time we've had a chance to bleach the cells. Also, I've signed off a few amnesties. Petty thieves, harmless idiots... MEZHNIKOV (mid 50s, fussy), the designer responsible for the
funeral, stands beside a frustrated Khrushchev (in a red
Now, curtains. Ruched or not ruched?
Ruched? What the fuck is ruched? Mezhnikov demonstrates with the material under the coffin.
Ruched. Not ruched. Ruched. Not ruched.
Why should I care? While you were hiding in your mama's linen closet I was at Stalingrad! I ate rats. Raw rats. Didn't even cook them!
Behind him, a nervous group of STEELWORKERS. Awkward,
Ah, the Steelworkers. Some REAL men! Lads, come, come!
Malenkov moves over to assert his authority. Beria spots a line of girls and moves over to them.
Iron and steel are the very foundation of...
(to Steelworkers) Iron? IRON? Derisory laughter.
Capitalism was built on iron. STEEL - is the 20th Century. The Soviet Union is made of steel! And these! Men! Made it!
Huge wave of suppressed applause, caught between joyous
approval of Khrushchev and the solemnity of the place.
Delov arrives with a group of sweet, very Russian-looking girls, aged 7-9.
You found my girl?
We can't find her, Comrade Minis... Comrade General Secretary. These are suggested alternatives.
(to Khrushchev) Find her!
I thought you bastards knew where EVERYONE was! Find her, camel-cock!
Pervak is stood awkwardly behind him with Maria Yudina, the pianist. Khrushchev turns and switches into chivalrous mode.
Maria Veniaminovna. Please. Forgive my language, but he IS... one of those. This...
He gestures towards Malenkov who holds out his hand but she's seen Stalin in his coffin. It hits her. Stalin!
Yes. Stalin.
Malenkov is left hanging. He joins in the solemn moment.
Sad times. Thank you for coming.
Oh, I can't TELL you... the intensity of emotion when I heard. General ZHUKHOV - 57, imposing, covered in medals - strides
to the coffin, booming greetings to officers in the hall. He's followed by some of his ENTOURAGE.
If it wasn't for this man, I wouldn't be here today. - He lent me his driver! Hah! (to Pervak) Pervak! Handsome as ever! Put you in a frock, I'd marry you myself!
(returning) Ah, the generalissimo.

So, what does a war hero have to do to get some lubrication round here? (grabs one out of Delov's hand) Christ! Where's this from, a Polish flamethrower? Tastes like PISS... Slow-mo on Zhukhov as he drawls out 'Piss' with the caption:
"FIELD MARSHAL ZHUKHOV, HEAD OF THE SOVIET ARMY". BACK TO NORMAL As the others talk, Mezhnikov shows Malenkov a choice of two portraits of him on boards to pick from. Zhukhov reaches the others.
Nikita! Sorry to hear about your wife's affair! (off his reaction) She hasn't had one yet but God, I like the look of her. Hah!
Yes, she is... pretty.
Zhukhov. So rare to see you away from a mirror.

Beria. Must be such a... (re coffin) ...difficult time for you, hiding your happiness at a promotion. (to Khrushchev) Who stood down the army? Why have my men been sent back to their barracks? I'm smiling but I'm very fucking furious.
(notices Maria) Perhaps Comrade Yudina should prepare those precious hands.
Maria, I am mortified I cannot escort you, but...
He kisses her hand.
How very friendly. You never kiss my hand anymore.
So lovely to see you again, Comrade
Again?
I taught his wife's niece to play.
Katya! Yes, we were both at the salon recital...
Oh, I LOVE the idea of you at a "salon". You have a party trick? Burping the alphabet?
She sets off, passing Beria who whispers at her.
So many... (mimes piano-playing) ...notes. Like your one to Stalin. Which I have.
She looks back as she walks away, rattled.
So who confined the army to barracks?
That nonsense was NOT signed off by the whole committee.
Ah, so it was only one idiot, not the full set.
Malenkov has chosen the portrait he likes. He joins them.
What's going on?
Talk of the devil.
(to Zhukhov) The decision was mine supported by Comrade Malenkov...
(not sure what they're discussing) We took a view. And made a decision...
We're discussing the city security. I know.
(about Malenkov's hair) Christ, did Coco Chanel shit on your head? (to Beria) Moscow will be boiling with people. Your pansy torturers won't cope, you know. That's a job for MEN.
Oh, I have PLENTY. NKVD AND the...
Interior troops. Merged now, into this "KGB"...
(kissy lips) "KGB". Even SOUNDS like a bunch of nancies.
We're controlling the crowd at source. All trains inbound to Moscow have been cancelled...
(interrupts) But... I'm the trains! I'm the fucking funeral, I... I should have been asked.
(to Khrushchev) Please, Sir. I need to go...
I think she needs the toilet.
(to Malenkov) You need the army here. Not Beria's Keystone Cops.
Maybe we should see who can mobilise first. Oh. It seems to be me...
He heads off before Zhukhov can answer.
(to Zhukhov) We've got this under control. (to Khrushchev) You: find me the right girl. (to little girl) (MORE)
And you: go to that lady over there and she'll take you to the toilet.
He sets off after Beria. Delov heads off with the girls.
I need to punch someone.
May I suggest...
Khrushchev nods at a stack of boards with a portrait of a very airbrushed Malenkov being carried past.
He looks like my auntie after two peach brandies.
(calls from by the doors) We're opening the doors!

A line of MEN IN COLOURFUL AIR FORCE/NAVY UNIFORMS take position at intervals from the door to the coffin and beyond. We hear a large CROWD coming up the stairs. They pour in through the doors, a tidal wave of grieving humanity.
EXT. HALL OF COLUMNS - DAY Our footage of queues outside the Hall of Columns.
Footage of crowds gathering all over Russia.
EXT. DINGY COURTYARD OUTSIDE MOSCOW - DAY
Our Kiev footage of people leaving their houses, traipsing down Moscow streets. We focus on a FAMILY; a mother, father and their two young children.
EXT. RAILWAY TRACKS - DAY
Poor people, many in tears, walk along tracks, towards a barrier. NKVD MEN with machine guns stand by. Their senior officer is FEDIN.
No trains to Moscow! No... go... go back to your homes...
The crowd of people turn around and trudge purposefully along the railway track. Among them is the family we saw in sc123.
EXT. HOUSE OF THE TRADE UNIONS - DAY
Our shots of crowds growing outside. FOOTAGE OF HUGE CROWDS SNAKING UP THE STAIRS.
INT. HOUSE OF THE TRADE UNIONS - DAY
Our footage of the queue filing past Stalin.
INT. HALL OF COLUMNS - BALCONY - DAY
Svetlana chatting to Bulganin and Mikoyan, who clearly have to be somewhere else but feel obliged to talk to her.
...Papa always spoke with great fondness of you two. "My hammer and sickle" he always used to say, although I was never sure which...
Never heard that before. How...gratifying.
I'd be the sickle. Sharp. Poor Comrade Bulganin here...well, see he even has a head like the blunt end of a ball hammer...
(off polite laughter) My father always used to say go to you two when I need advice. Oh.
..or help...
(a hand on each) "They are true and loyal men."
Any time. Any time at all. Sorry, we have to take our places.
Svetlana alone. She leans over a balustrade, watching the grieving crowd. Beria joins her.
This is... real. Nobody's making them do this.
In the crowd below: grandmothers sob; a sailor removes his hat out of respect but drops it. The crowd keep moving and he scrabbles his way back to try and get his hat.
I don't suppose you want a chair?
Beria signals to an NKVD man who approaches with a chair.
I haven't sat down since he died. He was too big. You have to stand. Yes.
(what?)
Beria signals "no". The NKVD man leaves with the chair.
Do you remember Yuri?
(no) I remember everyone. It's a gift.
I loved him.
(suddenly remembers) Yuri Zhednov! Yes, special to you, I know. Some unsuitable associates. Tragic, really. I'm so sorry.
I want you to bring him back.
(are you mad?) But, my chick, how?
You brought Molotov's wife back.
She was... I'd kept her.....
You're returning so many people to their homes. I want ONE person. Is that really too much?
A beat.
For you, I will attempt the impossible.
He opens his arms to her. She awkwardly steps into them. Thank you.
I hate seeing you sad.
(awkward) I hate that you hate seeing me sad.
They hug awkwardly.
INT. HALL OF COLUMNS - DAY
The crowd files past the coffin: ordinary citizens mingle with representatives from nations and regions carrying demarcated floral tributes. Andreyev and Sergei supervise the music from a balcony.
ARCHIVE MATERIAL 5.
FOOTAGE OF DELEGATIONS, FLORAL TRIBUTES, CEREMONIES ALL OVER SOVIET UNION, QUEUES, PEOPLE COMING UP STAIRS.
INT. HALL OF COLUMNS - DAY

OUR FOOTAGE of the committee: Molotov, Kaganovich, Bulganin, Khrushchev, Malenkov, Beria and, closest to the public, Mikoyan, who's crying. A large wreath nearly brushes his face. He sneezes. A film crew is recording.
(sotto) Fucking pollen. They should make gas bombs with this stuff.
Molotov, sobbing heavily, looks across, sees him.
I know. I know... The wreaths and bouquets are getting absurdly big. One - with
the message "From the grieving people of Uzbekistan" on it only just fits through the large double doorway.
(to Malenkov) I think that's actually larger than the region itself.
Malenkov's little involuntary laugh. Molotov sees,
(chiding Beria) Inappropriate.

The Uzbek bouquet makes its way past. On the staircase, some mitres bobbing up and down, some very worn. A group of ORTHODOX BISHOPS approaching in full regalia. Shocked whispers from committee members. Not Beria.
Jesus Christ! It's the bishops!
What are these bastards doing at MY FUCKING FUNERAL?
Did I dream the last 36 years? Are we doing the revolution again from the START?
The Church belongs where we left it. In a ditch.
Tell Mikoyan to sneeze on the bastards as they go past.
(to Malenkov) Did you invite them?
No!
Ask Beria if it was him.
Don't order me around. (to Bulganin) Ask Beria if he invited the fucking bishops.
(to Beria) Did you invite the fucking bishops? Yes.
Bulganin turns back, chewing over Beria's answer.
Bulganin nods to Malenkov.
Yes. He did.
I'm going to give everyone in Red Square a voucher permitting one kick each to his stupid face.
(to Bulganin) Is he asking for more delicious hay?
No. He said something quite complicated about a voucher system.
This is rehabili... Listen to me! This is rehabilitation of the most counter revolutionary elements! He can't do that without the committee.
(taking over from Bulganin) He says you can't do it without the committee. Which is true...
(interrupting) I can cancel house arrests under article 31.
Malenkov realises he is right.
(to Khrushchev) He can in fact cancel house arrests under article 31.
The camera crew is on them again. They all look as solemn as they can. The bishops pass the coffin, making the sign of the cross.
(to Malenkov) Tell him that I... Actually, swap... swap... No!
No!
KHRUSHCHEV MALENKOV
We can make it look like it's part of the ceremony.

A dignified Khrushchev steps forward. Malenkov motionless, staring at NAVY OFFICERS struggling to get a too-high wreath through the door. The officers back up, then advance solemnly in a squatting position. The wreath low enough to go through but still too wide. Khrushchev eases back into line.
(whispers to Khrushchev) If only someone had checked...

The navy officers back up again with the wreath, then walk in with it sideways and squatting. Suddenly, an angry voice, away from the crowd. Vasily, in full uniform, drunk, trying to get to the coffin. Arguing with SENIOR NKVD OFFICERS, one of whom has a loudhailer.
They're murderers! Murderers! Give me your loudhailer!...
From their balcony, Andreyev and Sergei see Vasily. Sergei turns up a dial and the music grows louder.
(whispers to Khrushchev) Did you forget to give him his sedatives?
...If you don't hand it over I'll shove it so far up your arse your farts will deafen Moscow! (to Committee) ...I KNOW YOU KILLED MY FATHER!
(whispers to Bulganin) Tell them to get the steel workers. (to Malenkov) We have to go!...
The whisper goes from Bulganin to Kaganovich to Molotov to Tarasov, who hurries off. Vasily, now restrained, getting louder.
I WILL AVENGE MY FATHER, YOU
BUTTONED-UP SACKS OF SHIT! Some of the crowd are starting to notice. Sergei raises the volume a bit more.
Don't record this!
INT. HALL OF COLUMNS - WAITING ROOM - DAY
A group of STEEL WORKERS chat and smoke, putting on new overalls and caps bearing the hammer and sickle. Some have medals. Tarasov runs in.
Quick! We need you now! NOW! The steel workers are confused - they're not ready.
Are you actually made of steel? Now! Move!
The NKVD bundle them outside as, frenzied, they put out cigarettes, haul on jackets and, in one case, trousers.
INT. HALL OF COLUMNS - DAY

The steel workers emerge in a rush, trying to look dignified. Some are still hopping along, getting dressed. Vasily struggling with officers. One has his hand over Vasily's mouth. A bite - ouch - he lets go. Beria, over his shoulder, sees the steel workers approach.
Move, you hairy-assed sluggards!
They all turn to go. The manoeuvre turns into hesitant chaos. Molotov's in front.
Liars and revisionists! You killed Stalin!
Molotov sets off and the others follow him. He's not setting a fast enough pace - those at the back bunch up.
Decorum, please..
Faster, you SNAIL.
(to Vasily, trying to be both quiet and loud) SHHHHHH! SHHHHHUSHH!

Pursued by the NKVD, Vasily rushes up some stairs. Beria breaks from the line, speed-walks until he's pretty much out of public view then breaks into a run and heads up the staircase. Khrushchev follows.
INT. HALL OF COLUMNS - SMALL DIGNITARIES ROOM - DAY
A quiet holding area. Some Communist LEADERS including ZHOU ENLAI, with their TRANSLATORS. Vasily rushes in.
Friends! There has been a terrible crime! Zhou EnLai's translator translates for the Chinese leader.
My father - they killed him. They got a child to cut out his brain and send it to Washington!
Zhou Enlai's TRANSLATOR stops, then carries on.
They're ruthless monsters and sodomites!
The translator is trying to get every nuance. An NKVD officer grabs Vasily and pull him away.
...sucking the cocks and balls of New York Zionist queers in petticoats...
The translator makes sure he does the insult justice.
INT. HALL OF COLUMNS - LANDING OR ADJOINING ROOM - DAY
The NKVD wrestle Vasily to the ground. Beria and Khrushchev arrive.
I'll make sure you all stand trial and get garotted by your own lookalikes in Red Square...
Vasily. It's me.
Beria kneels beside Vasily who tries to spit at him, but he's lying down, so the spit just drops back onto his face.
Syphilitic cannibals! Zoo animals..
I know about the dead hockey team. The pretence. The denial.
Vasily stops struggling. He's accepted defeat.
Freak weather. (calmly) I want to make a speech at the funeral.
(To Khrushchev) He wants to make a speech at the funeral.
Oh, MORE good news.
The Dignitaries from inside the room quietly file out, and past Vasily and Co.
I'm his son. (flaring up again, struggling) Let me speak, you howling chimps...
Zhukhov appears, pushes Vasily into the now-vacated dignitaries room and punches Vasily hard across the face, knocking out a tooth. Vasily falls to the floor, unconscious. Zhukhov pockets the tooth.
I'll stick it under my pillow tonight. May even make a ruble.
He smiles at Beria - that's how you sort things round here. Beria notices that Svetlana's watching a few yards away, distressed.
Lana! He's my godson. I won't let anything happen to him.
She turns and hurries off.
Something has already happened to him!
Lana! (to an NKVD man) Tell her I'll be with her in a moment.
The NKVD man sets off after Svetlana.
(grabs Beria, and makes sure no-one else is around in the room) Not yet! (close, angry) You're bringing the Church back? What next? Are the Romanovs coming?
Don't be hysterical. We are in a new reality.
Well don't you sound fancy? You locked half the nation up. Beat them. Raped them. Killed them.
Yes, and now I'm releasing them. I put so many in jail, there are so many to let go. You won't believe how many will be free. Millions! The revolutions continues...
Not true. You're just doing it to gain power. You bend and crack the truth, like it was a human body.
Oh, you want the "truth"? Let's start with you calling the blameless Polina Molotova a Zionist spy. In her own apartment. I heard you. And so did my men.
He taps his ear to indicate they were listening.
But she was found guilty. You FOUND her guilty.
Past tense. In those "old days" you pine for, Nicky, that dissonance would get you shot.
Khrushchev starts to speak, Beria cuts him off.
More truth: you called that slut doctor Timashuk to Stalin's bedside...
That was your idea!
You initiated it. Again, there are recordings. A woman who denounced thousands of innocent doctors.
Khrushchev is thrown.
We ALL signed off on those arrests.
You're old regime, Khrushchev. Counter-revolutionary. (MORE)
The man who fucked up the flowers and invited his bit-on-the-side whore to play at the funeral. Even though she swore to kill Stalin. Who's now dead.
What are you talking about?
Beria hands him Maria's note.
She wanted Stalin dead and she knows your family. It's lucky we now live in the new Soviet Union or you and your wife and children would now be a pile of dust on the
floor of a crematorium toilet.
Khrushchev reads the note. The colour drains from his face.
INT. HALL OF COLUMNS - STAIRS - DAY.
Khrushchev comes back down the stairs and into the Hall of Columns. He sees some bishops in the distance, calls to an NKVD guard.
Get those boyfriends of Christ out of my sight!
We see the Bishops being moved away. There is some applause coming from a section of the queue.
I see they agree with me. No, Comrade Khrushchev, it's because they've spotted him.
He gestures over to the applauding section of the crowd. We can just make out Beria walking past, enjoying the applause.
Start the trains. The people can come to Moscow!
Meanwhile Beria is enjoying his moment. He's with Kubulov.
The revolution continues! (to Kubulov) Now start the other one.
EXT. MOSCOW STREETS - DAY.
A black maria pulls up. Several confused PRISONERS, some beaten, some of whom we saw arrested earlier, get out. The van drives off.
EXT. RAILWAY TRACKS - NIGHT
The provincial train TRACKS we saw earlier. Crowds are camped in front of the NKVD and Fedin with pictures of Stalin, candles, flowers etc. A whistle blows.
For Moscow! Train to Moscow!
People rush forward.
INT. MOSCOW APARTMENT BUILDING - NIGHT

The distinctive design we saw earlier. The woman turns away from the window. She's heard something. The middle-aged man who was arrested has returned. He's been beaten. She rushes over and hugs him. His son sits awkwardly in a chair: it's the young man who betrayed him to the NKVD.
INT./EXT. TRAIN ON PLATFORM - DAY
CROWDS clamber onto the train, including the FAMILY WITH TWO CHILDREN we saw earlier. Excitement, people clambering on board. Train packed full.
INT. LUBYANKA - PRISON CELL - DAY A PRISONER and the NKVD Interrogator from sc 73. The cell

door opens and a SOLDIER points a gun. The Interrogator moves to allow a clearer shot at the Prisoner. But the Soldier shoots the Interrogator. The Prisoner's relieved moan. The Soldier shoots him too, and leaves. We hear O.S a succession of unlocking doors, yells and double-gunshots
INT. KREMLIN - LARGE STAIRCASE - LATE AFTERNOON
KHRUSHCHEV alone, slowly making his way up the stairs. He can hear piano music. Someone practicing. He is trying to find the source.
INT. KREMLIN - SMALL ROOM - LATE AFTERNOON
A small room full of images of Stalin, and some of Malenkov.
(holding out note) This your work, is it?
Cut to Maria sitting as if in an interrogation, but with piano nearby.
Why? Are there spelling mistakes?
(suddenly furious) Don't ever speak to me like that again or I'll break your fingers! (waving note) The ammunition you've given Beria. Linking us. Me, my family. Do you know what sort of man he is?
He's releasing people from prison...
Releasing people was my idea! He rapes children! Did you know that? He's murdered millions. Have you?
Have I what?
Raped children, murdered millions?

I do not rape children! (pauses, agitated) I am not Beria! I have... stood up against our enemies. I have been relentless in my defence of the nation and the party. And I have hated doing what I had to do.
Hated it. But Beria loves every minute. I'm the reformer, not him! (trying to calm down) Why did you write that note?
Stalin killed my parents, my friends, Zinaida Reich,
You were friends with Kuperchinski? Christ! Anyone else?!
He's furious. Maria tries to hide her fear.
I don't know. Rovik perhaps? We were friends.
Rovik?! How the fuck are you even still alive?
Comrade. Nikita Sergeyevich, you know me, I...
No I don't know you. Don't say I know you. But now Beria ties me to you, and you know how this works. They kill your parents, then your sister. And brother. And friends....
Yes. I know how you all work.
For a beat it feels like Khrushchev might slap her.
INT. BERIA'S HOUSE - BEDROOM - NIGHT/EARLY EVENING
Sparsely furnished. Vasily in vest and military trousers, practising his speech. Svetlana sits on the bed.
My father was a warm and mighty bear...
He was. He WAS...
That bear now lies cold. We, 170 million cubs, his weeping orphans. (MORE)
Russian cubs, Georgian cubs, Armenian cubs. Lithuanian cubs...
Do you list every nation?
Should I not?
There's a knock on the door. Beria comes in.
How's my precious guest?
Vasily looks at him flatly, clearly still mistrustful.
Thank you for looking after him.
Of course. (offers him a hip flask) I brought you something to calm your nerves.
Vasily hesitates. Beria takes a swig.
Vasily, I'm your godfather. I would never poison you. I'd arrange a plane crash or something.
(he laughs, they don't)
Your jokes are always so.. clenched.
Svetlana takes the flask from Vasily, gives it back to Beria. Vasily's about to protest but Svetlana's firm.
(to Vasily, at Beria) We agreed we would be presentable at the reception. Vasily sulkily agrees.
I hate being sober. It's a terrible, terrible mood to be in.
She kisses him and leaves with Beria, who's impressed.
INT. BERIA'S HOUSE - CORRIDOR - NIGHT/EARLY EVENING
Beria and Svetlana come out of the room and shut the door. They walk away, leaving an NKVD MAN on guard.
(indicates speech) Going to "reaffirm" your father's contract with the people. New rights. New liberties...
Why does everything have to be "new"?
They go through into...
INT. BERIA'S HOUSE - LIVING ROOM - NIGHT/EARLY EVENING
A comfortable living room - the nicest we've seen. Fireplace, rug, souvenirs and gifts all over. On a table, a massive file is open.
..."new" potatoes..."new" Spring
Fashions..."new"... Beria hands Svetlana a long, long list from the file, indicates an entry.
Yuri's dead. March 15th, 1949. Attempted escape from Kolyma with twelve others. Executed. I'm Sorry...
Svetlana bursts into tears, crumples.
But... you said you'd bring him back. You...
It wasn't possible...
You lied to me!...
It was outside my jurisdiction.
He indicates the list. Vasily suddenly enters the room, looking for the flask.
My mind's going everywhere, like a million clarinets! I need...
He's spotted the flask. Svetlana, furious, grabs it as Beria silently signals to the NKVD Man to escort Vasily back into the room and lock the door. Vasily miserably complies.
(ignoring Vasily, and reading the letter) Signed by the entire Committee.
(tenderly) Sit down, Svetlana.
I'd prefer to...
(kind, forceful) Sit down.
Svetlana refuses. She's angry, scared, defiant, pacing.
We had to do some terrible things for the Union. Your father said: Always look in the distance, never at the mud and blood at your feet. But, Svetlana, if Yuri were alive today I'd be releasing him.
So you're saying, all that... (indicates list) ...killing was wrong.
(impatient) Many lies were told. Evidence was fabricated. Those responsible will be found and...
Shot? This killing machine is never going to stop, is it?
Grow up, Svetlana! The world out there is full of dangerous men in uniforms and dinner suits. I'm offering you and that...gabbling bowl of moonshine my protection.
We are not children! (rethinks) I am not a child!
Oh you are. And I warn you, Svetlana. Stay by my side. Or you'll both be beaten inside out and strung up for the crows. By the others.
Why should I trust you?
Because I'm the only one who's telling you - trust no-one else.
Can I keep this?
Of course. Svetlana, I am so very sorry.
You're just like my father.

She kisses him tenderly on the cheek and leaves. He goes to a hidden DOOR and unlocks it. Goes up some dark and dusty stairs, to a small room. We see a mattress, a vase with flowers, and an awkward teenage girl, crying. He smiles at her.
Oh no. I'm here to cheer you up...
INT. KREMLIN RECEPTION - MAIN ROOM - NIGHT

A wall of noise. And bodies. It's the end of a dinner. The dignitaries have broken into groups to smoke, drink and chat. Maria's playing Chopin. We're with Khrushchev as he weaves his way round. Zhukhov is regaling DIGNITARIES with a war story.
The greatest enemy any soldier has to face? It's not guns, or fear, or hunger. It's chafing...
Svetlana is with Vasily who's smartened up and sobered up. She scans the room.
I've made enemies of everyone here. Oh Christ, sober's HARD.
(encouraging) You're doing well. I almost believe
that smile.
It's easy. All I have to do is say hello, shake their hands, and not call them cunts.
The HUNGARIAN PRESIDENT comes up to them, shakes hands. Vasily's about to say something... We're now with Beria as he enters the room, creating a gravitational pull. Back with Vasily and Svetlana.
All of Hungary sends you our deepest condolences. Anything you want from us, just ask.
Vasily, anything you want from Hungary?
A horse, I would like. Big horse.
Khrushchev comes into the room and looks around. DELOV comes up to him with a spotty, awkward TEENAGE GIRL.
Comrade Minister. I've found her.
Khrushchev looks at her, full of contempt.
She's too big! She looks like a baby giraffe fell down some stairs.
Delov takes her away. Kaganovich and Bulganin join Khrushchev.
Heard you were "canoodling" with the pianist?
Yeah, she give it plenty of left hand, Nicky?
Did she sit on your Presidium?
(ignoring them) This is classic Beria. Nail the woman as a spy, link her to the man, nail the man. I need someone with an army. Now!
Svetlana spots Mikoyan. He tries to avoid her gaze, but she's insistent.
Comrade Sickle! You look very dashing tonight!
(disdaining Vasily) Thank you. I hope your brother isn't armed this evening...
He's not even loaded! Fuck me!
Mikoyan pretends he's being called away. He's off.
"He's not even loaded"?
(joining Zhukhov) Comrade Field Marshall. A word?
I'm in the middle of a... Khrushchev steering Zhukhov to a door.
(to Zhukhov's companions) He either planted the flag on Hitler's bunker, or knocked the bear out with one punch. (to Zhukhov) There's an urgent military matter.
He guides him away.
Bloody rude, actually, Nicky. It wasn't Hitler's bunker, it was Goebbels'. And it genuinely happened.
They pass Bulganin, who's a little agitated.
What's going on, Nicky? They say the streets are heaving with people...
Zhukhov and Khrushchev exchange a glance. Back with Svetlana and Vasily. Molotov approaches, his arms reaching out.
Here's Molotov. A hundred rubles says he calls us 'comrades' then hugs us.
(to Svetlana and Vasily) Comrades!
(sotto) I'm not kissing him.
Khrushchev steering Zhukhov to out the doorway of the main reception and across the large stairway.
(conspiratorial) Could I count on you if someone we both know was out of control, dangerous...
(sotto) Yes! Wanted to deal with him for bloody ages. I'm in.
Khrushchev shows them into...
INT. KREMLIN - EMPTY ROOM - NIGHT (CONTINUOUS)
Khrushchev and Zhukhov are alone.
(that was easy) Really? Good. Because he's planning a coup.
What? Come on, Nicky. He's so pissed all the time he can't tell his arse from his windpipe. Khrushchev pauses.
We ARE talking about the same person. Yes?
Beria.
Beria? Well, someone's grown balls the size of Kremlin domes. Yes, bravo. Bloody Beria. How DARE that hookworm try to out-manouevre the army!
He wants to hijack my reforms, make the people love him...
The people are a pendulum of idiots. They can be made to hate him... (clicks fingers) Like that. But listen, Nicky... (closer) I've got the soldiers. What have you got..?
All I've got is the funeral, and flowers and fucking trains. A beat. Khrushchev picks up a phone.
(down the phone) Get me the Transport Minister... Zhukhov slamming doors, opening cupboards.
You'd think there'd be vodka. Even beancounters drink vodka...
(shushing him) Double the train services into Moscow.
They head back the stairway to the reception.
Moscow's full already. It'll be total bloody chaos. Well played, Comrade.
I'm the peacemaker round here and I'll fuck up anyone who gets in my way!
Cock the size of an atomic bomb.
INT. KREMLIN RECEPTION - MAIN ROOM (CONTINUOUS)
Maria at the piano, playing. Beria leans in and whispers, cleaning his glasses.
So what does Nikita taste like? I assume it's shitty traitor, like you. What do YOU taste of, I wonder?
She stops playing. Dramatic silence.
I will carry on if you step away
from the piano.
(innocent, to the room) I always thought that piece ended in an odd place!
Beria steps back from Maria and she carries on playing. Malenkov is with Delov and a LITTLE GIRL who looks very like the one from the picture.
Smile, sweetheart. The girl reveals terrible teeth.
With your mouth shut? (off her closed mouth)
Now I must take my dog out. For an evacuation! Perhaps two!
Molotov leaves.
(you're the biggest) When this is over, go home and drink vodka until you pass out. I never want to see you sober again.
EXT. NARROW MOSCOW STREETS - NIGHT

We cut from street to street, and see them fill with people. Wider shots reveal them coming in all directions. Hordes of people on narrow streets, getting very crowded. An NKVD colonel, ENTIN is having a quiet cigarette. He looks up. At the end of the street, the crowd are heading towards him. He's on a barricade made of trucks and armoured cars with gun turrets, manned by about 20 NKVD SOLDIERS. Entin motions for the loudhailer. It's passed to him. He addresses the crowd.
Halt! This is a secure area! Return to your homes, comrades! Now!
The crowd hesitate but don't stop. Some hold images of
(shouts) We've come to see Comrade Stalin.
Others in the crowd roar approval.
Please, stop! Or we will have to open fire! They're still coming. The streets are jammed, heaving. People
straining, crying, children being passed over heads.
(to his soldiers) On my order, fire over their heads.
The soldiers take aim. One of them shoots in panic. Panic in the crowds.
I have not given the command! Shrieking. Bodies being lifted. Some pick up rocks and stones from the street.
Disperse! Go back!...
The crowd begin to advance. Entin draws his revolver. People start throwing rocks. One hits Entin. He staggers back, bleeding. The crowd is almost upon them. FIRE!
ENTIN (CONT'D)
The soldiers and machine guns fire. The soldiers reload and keep firing, panicking. The screams of the crowd mix with the terrifying gunshots.
CEASE FIRE! CEASE FIRE!
It stops. Entin stares out, horrified. His POV: piles of bodies, mostly dead, some wounded: men, women, children.
INT. KREMLIN RECEPTION/STAIRCASE - MAIN ROOM - NIGHT

NOTE: THROUGH THIS SCENE, AS NEWS OF THE STREET DISTURBANCES SPREAD, THE ROOM GRADUALLY EMPTIES AS OFFICIALS ARE SEEN BEING INSTRUCTED TO HANDLE THE SITUATION. Beria approaches Malenkov, followed closely by Khrushchev. Malenkov is practicing for the photo, holding the girl up.
What's going on?
(through smile) That moron put the trains back on. Rioting crowds. Fifteen hundred dead.
(putting the girl down) No, you're too heavy. She's put down and taken away.
Beria's men fired on the crowds. He's out of control, Georgy, I...
(to Khrushchev) What's between your ears? Sausage?
Oh, I think I know who the people will blame. (indicates self) The poor old shitsack in charge...
Georgy, look...
Delov, bring the girl back. I need to make an appearance.
Perhaps the army should be in charge of the railway stations.
Delov brings the girl back as Zhukhov arrives.
Bit late now, eh Beria?
They were outside Moscow, on his orders!
My orders? They were... (points with girl's hand) ...your orders! And starting the trains... (pointing at Khrushchev) ...were his orders!
So, what are YOU going to do?
(to girl) What do you think, darling? You're the only one making any sense.
They all gradually edge out the room to a quieter spot by the staircase.
Someone is going to have to be the face of this fucking travesty.
That's easy: Khrushchev. No!
You ordered the trains!
And your men shot the people getting off them.
He does have a point.
No he doesn't. There is no point to Nikita Khrushchev.
Malenkov signals - The girl is taken away again.
We should blame the security forces. Hothead officers on the ground...
(to Malenkov) God Almighty, can you THINK? Or can you only pose for portraits? I. Am. Security. You... (mimicking Malenkov's openmouthed surprise) ...baked cod in a haircut.
Oh, says the fucking jellied eel...
Let's all calm down. (to Malenkov) Even if you DO get blamed. We close ranks. We rehabilitate you, we...
Yes, it's the new Soviet Union now...
For the good of the party I ask...

You spineless rat! Fuck you! You're just a bloated corpse we propped up and stuck in a fancy uniform. (to the room) You want to blame me? It's time all of you realised who kept the daggers out of your fucking backs! Show some fucking respect! I have documents on all of you. ALL OF YOU! (to Khrushchev) 42 dead, 173 exiled. Your signature. 5th October 1946. (MORE)
Katzenelson. 63 executed, exiled. Your signature...
Beria. Enough.
(to Kaganovich) Radek, Skrypny, your own brother. Your signature...
He gestures at Khrushchev, Kaganovich, Bulganin and Mikoyan. Malenkov is stood behind him.
All of you! I've seen what you've done. It's a very! Fucking! Long! List!
He scatters some papers on the floor. Silence. The committee stare at the papers.
(heading off) I think I saw some brandy out here...
Lavrentiy. We can put this right.
(calming down) We'll meet straight after the funeral tomorrow. We will find out who's properly to blame for this. Arraign the officers who fired. Yes? Of course.
Beria heads down the stairs. The others stand there, still stunned from Beria's outburst.
We can just demote him. Make him Minister Responsible for Fisheries or something.
Do you know what you're saying?
He's using you, Georgy. He'll use you and then he'll kill you. You saw those papers.
He pointed at you, not me.
He said "all of you".
I was behind him. He went (gestures) "all of you".
"All of you".
No. "All of you".
(demonstrates) He said: "All of you".
All of YOU can kiss MY arse! Get nme the girl, I'm going onto the balcony.
EXT. KREMLIN - BALCONY - NIGHT
Malenkov steps onto a balcony with the girl. Because he doesn't lift her up, we can hardly see her.
EXT./INT. OPEN SPACE SURROUNDED BY WOODLAND/CAR - EARLY MORNING
Two cars parked, with their drivers some distance away. Kaganovich and Khrushchev lean against one of the cars.
Who will the people blame? Me or Beria? Who looks like he's got blood on his boots?
(you?) Oh, Beria. People will definitely blame Beria. MURDERER!
Molotov approaches on foot, with a small dog.
You've killed hundreds, Nicky! You certainly got your big funeral!
I regret this already..
(interrupts) Get in the car. What?
Can't talk. Get in the car. Khrushchev and Kaganovich share a look - then get in.
Molotov pulls his dog in too.
(RE dog) I see she moults.
Molotov takes out a cuddly toy. The dog starts barking loud, irritating - and jumping up, trying to grab it.
This- (waves the cuddly toy again, to more barks) -is for any hungry ears...
The car isn't bugged. We'd see wires stretching to Moscow.
(looks out) The drivers. So... (close, menacing) Let's do him in. What?

(vigorously shakes the toy) Beria. The treacherous snake brought back the Bishops. And he brought back Polina. He expressly ignored Stalin's orders. The man has no soul. He ordered his men to open fire. Genius, Nicky.
(just about keeping up with Molotov's logic) Yes, Beria's the murderer here...
Those who were killed must not die in vain...
This is how the Soviet Union was built. Not with Bishops. Today, Beria gets an eight-foot crucifix up his arse! The dog's still yapping.
I've had nightmares that make more sense than this.
The people who died are martyrs. You have to do it in... The dog has stopped barking.
it jumps and barks. Molotov waves the toy again and
Please. Can this fucking DOG be a martyr?
You have to do it in one go. Bang! Like in '36. Won't be easy though.
Zhukhov's already got the army sharpening their bayonets...
And it's got to be today. Only time the army's in the capital.
Excellent. I'm in!
Good, good. We have a majority! Yes!
You two, me, Bulganin...
(cuts in) You need Malenkov and Mikoyan too.
But... why?

It's what Stalin would have wanted. The Committee, as one! Act fast Comrade. Act fast or be dead. (to dog) YES. Uncle Nicky's going to be dead if he doesn't get a move on, isn't he? Oh yes he is. Yes he is...
INT. MAUSOLEUM - DAY The committee, dignitaries, generals, Svetlana, Mezhnikov and
Kobulov wait in formal dress. The guarded coffin is nearby. Khrushchev watches uneasily as Malenkov jokes with Beria. Svetlana comes close.
You promised to protect me and my family...
Of course. Always, my dearest most precious...
Well you didn't.
She slips him the piece of paper Beria gave her, leaves. Beria and Zhukhov appear, surprising Khrushchev.
So. Comrade Zhukhov and I have been conferring...
Khrushchev looks appalled. Has he been betrayed?
All chums again now, eh? Onward!
He's come up with a plan for Vasily's speech.
It will go ahead but it "may" coincide with the fly-past.
(flooded with relief) Hah! Excellent! That's an excellent idea! Zhukhov! The genius!
Let's give the old maniac the sendoff he deserves.
A general sense of bonhomie. Beria puts his hand on Khrushchev - it's a reconciliation. He then pats Khrushchev's trousers... Bang, it's the tomato game again. Zhukhov finds it funny.
It's what he would have wanted.
Kobulov gestures to Beria who goes over to him, leaving Malenkov with Khrushchev.
A 21-gun salute begins outside - the signal for the funeral.
We have to act today.
Let's bury the old man first.
Khrushchev is about to reply but is interrupted by Mezhnikov.
Comrades! Please take your places for the cortege. Khrushchev and his allies take their places around the
coffin. Beria and Malenkov are at the front, next to Khrushchev. Zhukhov is directly behind him.
Malenkov joins in, as before, expecting Zhukhov to allow him to command the moment.
Yes, lift, everyone.
Everyone lifts and tries to get comfortable. Some have a shoulder under it, others just a hand. They start walking the coffin forward towards the open door as a cannon fires every few seconds.
(to Malenkov) Georgy! Georgy!
EXT. RED SQUARE - EMERGING FROM THE MAUSOLEUM - DAY
They shuffle out into a solemn Red Square, packed with mourners. Zhukhov still pressing Khrushchev.
Is Malenkov on board?
Georgy's in. One hundred per cent.
All or nothing now, Nicky, eh?

They are now in full view of the crowd. The pall-bearers shift the coffin on into the mausoleum, helped by some SOLDIERS. Zhukhov takes a step back and salutes. We see an extreme long shot of Zhukhov saluting. His eyes flick upwards. He knows he's being watched. A colonel with bushy eyebrows - BREZHNEV, on a Red Square rooftop, puts down his binoculars. Khrushchev nods at Bulganin and Kaganovich: it's happening.
EXT. MAUSOLEUM - BALCONY - DAY The balcony on top of Lenin's mausoleum. The committee
members and dignitaries are stood in a line.
(struggling, in tears) Stalin's love of ...love of the nation... The people's pain... The pain of the people... Oh...
We pan along legs. Chalk marks - initials of who's to stand where. A technician crouches out of view. Khrushchev's foot scratches an itch on his calf.
INT. CORRIDOR FROM MAUSOLEUM TO KREMLIN - DAY
Brezhnev and MOSKALENKO walk with briefcases towards some NKVD MEN by a set of double doors. An NKVD lieutenant, Pervak salutes them. They show papers, hand over pistols, go inside.
EXT. MAUSOLEUM - BALCONY - DAY
Malenkov's delivery is incredibly dull.
Yet we will draw strength from the continuity of the revolution as it goes on an on, generation after generation... yet today we pause in grief, and sorrow. But is not a
pause in itself part of the
Beria looks bored. Others sway to keep warm.
INT. KREMLIN - TOILETS - DAY
Pictures of Stalin and Lenin. Brezhnev closes the door. Both men open up their briefcases to reveal wires, a small red light bulb, electrical equipment.
EXT. MAUSOLEUM - BALCONY
Vasily is now at the microphone. He shouts into it.
My father was a great social scientist! But we, the people of the Soviet Union, are not laboratory animals! No! We are laboratory technicians!
The very noisy fly-over begins. He tries to shout over it.
My father! Your father! Our father!
INT. KREMLIN - TOILETS - DAY

Moskalenko fixes the lightbulb to a window frame. Brezhnev trails a wire along the edge of the skirting board towards a set of double-doors into a larger meeting room, with chairs around a long oak table.
EXT. MAUSOLEUM - BALCONY - DAY
Beria speaking, Malenkov and Khrushchev on either side.
We must embrace our terrible loss with the strength and love for our nation that Stalin himself..
INT./EXT. KREMLIN - MEETING ROOM/COURTYARD - DAY

Moskalenko hammers a wedge into the window frame, checks it's jammed shut, as NKVD GUARDS listen to Beria's speech in a courtyard outside, their backs to the window. Brezhnev fastens a button underneath the table, checks a wire leading down the table leg and under the carpet. He puts a place card - "Comrade General Secretary Malenkov" at the head of the table, where the button is.
EXT. MAUSOLEUM - BALCONY - DAY
Beria continues his speech.
We now carry forward the torch of revolution. I applaud the Central
Committee, who have declared Georgy
and General Secretary...
INT. KREMLIN - TOILETS/MEETING ROOM - DAY
Brezhnev presses the button under the table. Moskalenko is by the red bulb. It lights up.
EXT. MAUSOLEUM - BALCONY - DAY
The committee members are feeling the cold.
..We promise you rights and liberties, bread and peace.
Everyone in the crowd applauds. A bit too much for Malenkov.
EXT. RED SQUARE - DAY
The view from the balcony of Red Square - the applauding crowd, the flags, the banners and portraits.
INT. KREMLIN CORRIDOR/MEETING ROOM - DAY
Beria walks cheerfully with Molotov, followed by the PRESIDIUM MEMBERS, Zhukhov - in army greatcoat - then Khrushchev and Malenkov.
"Bread and peace". I knew it would work. It was between "peace" and "sausages".
The generals and ZHUKHOV reach the NKVD guards at the door.
I think we can rely on the liberators of Berlin to use their weapons appropriately. Khrushchev peels off.
Just going to freshen my trousers.
INT. KREMLIN - TOILETS - DAY
Khrushchev heads into the toilet. Brezhnev and his men are all there, waiting quietly. Nobody says anything. Khrushchev pulls out Svetlana's Yuri note, soiled with tomato.
INT. KREMLIN CORRIDOR/MEETING ROOM - DAY
Khrushchev catches up with Malenkov in the corridor ahead of the meeting room. Khrushchev discreetly hands Malenkov Svetlana's note.
Beria gave this to Svetlana. Let her know what you did to Zhednov. See? He'd shit on all of us.
Malenkov grows pale. He quickly puts the paper in his pocket.
The snake. Alright.
I'll bring up the emergency motion. There's a button under the desk at your end. You hit it. Yes?
Malenkov nods, nervously.
Yes. But just a demotion.
INT. KREMLIN - TOILETS - DAY
Zhukhov enters the toilet. Opens his coat to reveal Kalashnikovs. Brezhnev smiles. A presidium member walks in to go to the toilet.
I didn't do any...
A hand is clamped over the Presidium member's mouth.
INT./EXT. KREMLIN - MEETING ROOM/COURTYARD - DAY
Malenkov is at the head of the table, Beria near the window. Outside, some NKVD GUARDS patrol the courtyard. Malenkov.
Malenkov looks at him, unable to say anything.
Let's start. (still nothing. He takes over) SHALL WE? Item One. The unfortunate
events of yesterday evening...
Khrushchev raises his hand. Malenkov doesn't acknowledge him.
It is clear that the regrettable deaths of many citizens hastening to the capital...
Khrushchev thrusts his hand even higher, glares at Malenkov.
...had several local causes...
Er, Comrade Khrushchev has the floor...
Beria, perplexed, looks at Malenkov, who avoids his gaze.
Comrades, I propose a new Agenda Item One. "The conduct of Comrade Beria".
Can anyone else hear that barking dog? I've lost my thread now...
Malenkov just stares at the table in front of him.
I accuse Comrade Beria of centralising power within his Ministry at the expense of the Party and the Central Committee. Of betraying the Soviet Union to capitalist interests...
(to everyone) I warn you, Nicky. This petulant Wild West stuff will end very badly.
(indicating button) Georgy! Georgy!
Malenkov just stares forward, unable to move.
Comrades, Perhaps there is concern at the speed of our reforms. But really, THIS nonsense?
Khrushchev leaps up, rushes round to the button and hits it hard, knocking Malenkov out of the way.
Arrest this madman! He's out of control!
INT. KREMLIN - TOILETS - DAY
The red light bulb lights up.
Good luck, ladies!
INT./EXT. KREMLIN - MEETING ROOM/COURTYARD - DAY
Soldiers burst into the room, led by Zhukhov.
Hands in the air! Beria puts the palm of his hand on the window. The NKVD guards are too far away to see it.
EXT. KREMLIN - COURTYARD - DAY
On Beria's palm, he's written: "ALERT".
INT./EXT. KREMLIN - MEETING ROOM/COURTYARD - DAY
Zhukhov points his weapon at Beria.
Hands in the air or bullet in the fucking face!
Malenkov's hands go up. Khrushchev slaps them down in disbelief.
(banging on window) GUARDS!!...
Zhukhov hits him in the face with his rifle butt. Beria drops to the ground, his pince-nez fly off. Zhukhov gets in close.
See? You want a job done properly, send for the army.
He frisks Beria. The other soldiers keep their guns on him.
He's got a knife by his ankle. Beria stares at Malenkov, who starts to cry.
INT. KREMLIN - MEETING ROOM - DAY
Beria is handcuffed on a chair, his face a bloodied mess. Georgy...
Malenkov, sat across the room, looks ashamed.
(to Malenkov) Say nothing.
Remove his belt. It's hard for a man to escape when his trousers are falling down.
Leave the man some dignity, please. That suit without a belt would be a disaster!
Gentlemen, rest assured I sense the strength of feeling in the room.
Shut up, you fucking tumour.
That man is responsible for the deaths of hundreds of thousands of innocents...
We all are...
Exactly. Collective guilt.
Comrades, please...
What happens now?
Zhukhov mimes shooting Beria.
Zhukhov! (to Malenkov) We wait for Red Square to empty of NKVD...
Then I give the signal and we roll this barrel of shit out.

The doors burst open. It's Kobulov and 2 NKVD lieutenants and Pervak, armed and carrying trays of sandwiches and cold meats. They freeze. Everyone turns and looks at them. Zhukhov clamps his hand over Beria's mouth.
Sorry, comrades. Wrong room.
They turn to go. Beria tries to call after them.
Those sandwiches were curling up anyway. (to Beria) Your lot can't even do CATERING. (MORE)
(to room) Let's go.
EXT. KREMLIN - SIDE ENTRANCE - DAY

Several trucks and armoured cars drive up. Soldiers leap out and surround the NKVD, who raise their weapons. An army COLONEL walks over to an NKVD OFFICER. The NKVD officer gestures for the NKVD to lower their guns. The colonel hurries inside, followed by his men, guns cocked.
INT. MOSCOW APARTMENT BUILDING - NIGHT
The middle-aged man arrested earlier is playing chess with his son. All is forgiven. The NKVD burst in.
No! NO! He didn't mean anything!
The NKVD march over to the son, grab him and head out.
INT. KREMLIN - CORRIDORS/STORE CUPBOARD - DAY
The colonel and his men walk through. Kobulov spots them. Dives into a store-cupboard. The men head in after him. We hear shots.
INT. BERIA'S HOUSE - BEDROOM - DAY

Vasily lies on the bed in his ceremonial uniform, drinking. The door is opened by an NKVD OFFICER, who steps to one side. An army GENERAL, a CAPTAIN and two SOLDIERS enter. The soldiers aim their rifles at Vasily.
No! NO! You can't!...
The general fires his pistol into the wall by Vasily's head.
You nearly fucking killed me and now I'm DEAF! You hear me, I'm DEAF!
The captain hauls Vasily up, tears off his epaulets, his medals, buttons. He's been disgraced. They drag him out.
Do you know who I am? Do you know who YOU are? Who the fuck are you?
INT. BERIA'S HOUSE - LIVING ROOM - DAY

Some NKVD OFFICERS and Beria's terrified STAFF are being marched out at gunpoint by a group of soldiers. Vasily's dragged into the living room. The frightened teenage girl is hauled out of the hidden room. MARIA is also taken out of another locked room.
INT./EXT. KREMLIN - MEETING ROOM/COURTYARD - DAY
Zhukhov peeks through the curtains. The NKVD have been replaced by Red Army SOLDIERS. Zhukhov moves across to Beria and stands him up. Takes off his belt.
OK, Judy Garland. Let's go.
He hauls him forward. Beria's trousers start to fall. Brezhnev pulls them up for him and Beria grabs them.
If we could discuss this rationally...
(that's a good idea) You're right. We should... (gags Beria) "Ooh, what's happening? My trousers are round my ankles but my cock's not inside an orphan..."
One of the newly-arrived soldiers hands Zhukhov a sack. He puts it over Beria's head.
Hey! Nicky! We agreed. A demotion.
Oh, it's definitely THAT, wouldn't you say?
Remember when you took Polina away? How you must have enjoyed it. I'm certainly enjoying this, you cockroach.
Beria struggles. Zhukhov punches him in the gut. He folds but is held up by Moskalenko and Brezhnev. His trousers fall. Brezhnev pulls up Beria's trousers, puts them in his hands.
Hold them!...
(taunting Beria) How's the meeting going, would you say, COMRADE SACK? You're on a list now, fucker!
Stop this!...
I'M THE REFORMER!
Beria is led out the room, battling with his trousers.
Roudenko is in the basement, along
You're putting him on trial? Now? I'm disgusted! And slightly
This is all totally, totally, beyond any agenda...
Listen to yourself. What do you think is happening here, Georgy? Should someone be taking minutes?
We have to act instantly...
Molotov bangs the table.
Bang! Over! Marxism-Leninism was born in flames! Let the fire of our vengeance consume all traitors and bastards!
No! I forbid it!...
You want the NKVD to spring him from jail? Things'd get a bit lively then...
Nobody wants civil war.
He will kill us all... Khrushchev tries to hand Malenkov a document.
Come on Georgy. Sign this or we're all dead.
He deserves a trial! The man deserves a trial!...
(furious) What about Tukhachevsky and

Mikhoels? Didn't they deserve a trial? And poor Sokolnikov. Who begged him to look after his elderly mother. That monster strangled her in front of him. I've killed a lot of people. I did it because it was my duty. That human turd killed for pleasure.
Khrushchev puts a brotherly hand on Malenkov's shoulder.
Georgy, we've gone too far now. The only choice we have is between his death or his revenge.
Malenkov looks at him, pale and dazed.
I'm General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union.
That's right. You are.
He hands him the document and a pen. Malenkov signs it.
EXT. KREMLIN - BACK AREA - DAY
From a distance we see the generals and Brezhnev dragging Beria. His trousers fall and he trips.
INT. KREMLIN - WINDOWLESS ROOM - DAY
A bleak space. An improvised courtroom. On a raised stage: ROUDENKO, mid-40s in a judge's gown. Next to him, Marshall KONEV.

The Presidium and Committee members are there with senior PARTY AND MILITARY OFFICIALS. Armed SOLDIERS everywhere. Zhukhov, Moskalenko and Brezhnev walk Beria to the improvised dock. Zhukhov removes the sack and gag.
You are all witnesses to a criminal travesty! Think what you like! You are STILL witnesses! Konev keeps banging.
I demand my rights under, under... article 43 of the... Stop banging, I demand to be heard! We are not savages!
YOU are! YOU are!
(weakening, to Malenkov) Georgy... Our wives... what will you tell your wife? And mine? The children?
Comrade General Secretary...
Trembling, Malenkov looks to Khrushchev who nods. Malenkov tries to read out a statement.
"Lavrenti Pavolovich Beria..."
You fucking traitor...
"Lavrenti Pavlovich Beria. You are accused..."
Malenkov stops. He looks like he's going to faint.
Fucks sake! (taking the document) "You are accused of using your position as Minister of the Interior to plot against the Soviet Union with the goal of forwarding the interests of foreign powers..."
(at same time) I am a Marshall of the Soviet Union, a member of...
Foreign powers! Which ones? The fucking MOON?
(over Beria) "You are also accused of 347 counts of rape, of sexual deviancy and bourgeois immorality, of acts of perversion with children as young as 7 years old...
Ah. Everyone in this ROOM is responsible for someone's death. So let's find something exotic for old Beria.
Beria stops protesting, completely knocked. He spots Svetlana, her eyes full of hate.
Svetushka...
(reading as quickly as he can) ...including the rapes of Luba Dolomaya, aged 12, Petra Nikova, 13 ...
YOU ARE THE RAPISTS! ERROR! ERROR! ERROR!
Khrushchev skips ahead, trying to shout over Beria.
Anna Laranskaya, Nadia Ranova...etc etc... Magya Holovic...treason... anti-Soviet behaviour...er, the court finds you guilty and sentences you to be taken to Lubyanka prison and shot...
NO! NIKITA! I'M YOUR FRIEND!!
(whispers to Beria) Shut it. Look, here's "Exhibit A".

Beria looks down. Zhukhov is holding a knife to his ribs. Beria suddenly loses it, starts screaming like a little girl. Khrushchev calmly waves goodbye. Zhukhov, Brezhnev and some soldiers drag him outside. Chairs are knocked over, there are screams and shouts. It's chaos. They've got Beria as far as the doorway. A shot rings out. Then another. More screams. A burst of sub-machine-gun fire. A shout of "CEASE-FIRE!"
INT. KREMLIN - WINDOWLESS ROOM DOORWAY - DAY (CONTINUOUS) 186
Beria's body is in the doorway of the courtroom. He's riddled with bullets, bleeding heavily. Svetlana spits on the body.
For Yuri. For my mother.
Zhukhov reaches into Beria's mouth and pulls out a tooth.
(off their looks) Nearly a full set now.
EXT. KREMLIN - BACK AREA - DAY (CONTINUOUS)

Beria's body is hauled out by SOLDIERS. They dump it unceremoniously, douse it with petrol and set fire to it. Everyone emerges from the building, registering the burning corpse with varying levels of coolness. They've seen some brutal things. But Svetlana is shocked, and moves away from the group. She looks like she may throw up. Khrushchev is now the centre of gravity. Everyone wants to be in orbit around him. Grim wordless exchanges. Kaganovich is close
Now we can turn the corner. Put the bloodshed behind us.
ANGLE on Malenkov. He looks bereaved.
I worry about Malenkov though. Can we trust him?
Can you ever trust a weak man?
Khrushchev agrees. They can't. He spots Svetlana, walks over to her. Kaganovich acting as informal bouncer, keeping everyone else away.
I'm scared.
There's no need.
I don't know where...
Vasily's safe. I give you my word, Svetlana, I will look after him.
He hands her an envelope.
Here is your passage to safety, too...
(re envelope contents) Vienna?
Vasily must stay. For his own safety. We can't have a drunken madman spreading conspiracy theories all over the world... I could...
No. He stays. You must go, for YOUR sake. There will be a reckoning here. Your...story may not fit. One of these men might have to kill you...
She's shocked. Khrushchev needs her to look at him. She does.
I might have to kill you.
A stunned beat. Her bitter smile. She looks at Malenkov, Mikoyan, Bulganin...
I never thought it would be you.
Safe travels, Svetlana.
A bow, he rejoins the group. Someone has produced vodka. The corpse burns on.
Now we can turn the corner. Put the bloodshed behind us.
They place a large electric fan next to the bin.
I worry about Malenkov though. Can we trust him?
Can you ever trust a weak man?
(nods agreement) No...
He turns the fan on and tips the ashes out. We watch the ashes dispel in the wind.
INT. RADIO MOSCOW - MAIN AUDITORIUM

Maria is playing a Mozart piano concerto. CLOSING CARDS: 1) AFTER BERIA'S EXECUTION IN 1953, THE CENTRAL COMMITTEE TOOK CONTROL OF THE SOVIET UNION 2)IN 1956 KHRUSHCHEV MOVED TO DEMOTE OTHER MEMBERS OF THE COMMITTEE, INCLUDING MOLOTOV AND MALENKOV We pan up to a box, where we see Khrushchev smiling, in his element. Nina and some sycophants sit around him. We see Andreyev and Sergei in the control room. Something to suggest Andreyev's living in a new reality too - more anxious, surrounded by new equipment? Or more confident, bullying? COMMANDER IN CHIEF. We stay on Khrushchev smiling. A young Brezhnev is behind him.
